text: 《Record of Conversations》 [唐] Zhao Lin Volume One, Palace Department Liu Jiayu, the concubine of Emperor Xuanzong, gave birth to Yan Prince Bīn. Every time Emperor Suzong saw the prince, he would tell his attendants, "I and the prince are even more intimate among brothers, as all the maternal families are nobles of Guanzhong." Liu Jiayu was the daughter of Fan, the Right Chancellor of the Board of Works, and the sister of Qi Wu, the Prefect of Muzhou.

Emperor Suzong, while in the position of Crown Prince, once accompanied the various princes to visit the Taqing Palace with Emperor Xuanzong. A dragon was seen on the eastern beam of the palace hall. Emperor Xuanzong pointed at it and asked the princes, "Did you see anything?" They all replied, "No." He then asked the Crown Prince, who lowered his head without answering. The emperor asked, "Where is its head?" The Crown Prince replied, "In the east." The emperor patted him and said, "Truly my son." When An Lushan came to court, Crown Prince Li Heng repeatedly spoke of An Lushan's disloyal behavior, but Emperor Xuanzong remained silent. One day, the emperor summoned the Crown Prince and other princes to play ball. The Crown Prince secretly planned to injure An Lushan with saddles and horses. Emperor Xuanzong privately told the Crown Prince, "I am not without suspicion, but this barbarian has no tail, so you might as well leave him alone." Princess Zhenghe, the third daughter of Emperor Suzong, was married into the Liu family. During a banquet in the palace, there was a performance involving actors playing the roles of officials. The character dressed in green and holding a tablet was known as the "Shangjun" role. At the end of the Tianbao era, the Turkic general Abosi was executed according to the law, and his wife was assigned to the imperial harem. She was skilled in performing and was thus assigned to the musicians. On that day, she became the leader of the actors playing officials. The performance amused the emperor and those attending the banquet, but Princess Zhenghe alone lowered her head, frowned, and did not look up. The emperor asked her why, and she advised, "There are many attendants in the palace. Why must we have this person? If Abosi truly was a traitor, his wife is also a criminal and should not be close to the emperor's seat. If he was indeed wronged, how could we bear to make his wife a subject of ridicule among the performers? I may be foolish, but I deeply feel this is not acceptable." The emperor also felt pity and sorrow, so he stopped the performance and released Abosi's wife. From this, the princess was highly regarded.

The Empress Du of Emperor Daizong passed away. She was posthumously honored as Empress Zhenyi and is about to be buried. The Duke of Fenyang, the Honorable Father, was in Bi Prefecture, and because his son, Prince Consort De, is a consort of the imperial family, he wished to pay tribute. However, there is no precedent for a subject paying tribute to a queen. The Duke asked his attendants, and they all said, "There is no precedent for a subject paying tribute to a queen." The Duke said, "This matter needs to be decided by Counselor Liu." At that time, my maternal great-grandfather, Counselor of the Palace, was serving and was in charge of the Duke's correspondence. He was on a mission in the capital and immediately wrote to summon him. After he arrived, the Duke greeted him with a smile and said, "There is an urgent matter that requires your expertise." He then explained the matter of the tribute. Counselor Liu initially agreed with the others, but then he said, "Rituals are based on human feelings. Your Excellency's merit and virtue are different from those of ordinary people, and moreover, you are related to the imperial family. Starting from Your Excellency, it is also appropriate." The Duke said, "It perfectly aligns with your original intention." Counselor Liu wrote the tribute text, and the first part of his official title reads, "Prince Consort De's father." The middle part explains the special favor allowing the tribute. The words are concise and the rituals are complete. The Duke was very pleased after reading it. The text is listed below:

On a certain day in a certain month of a certain year, the father of Princess Guo Ai, who holds the titles of Vice Marshal of Guanxi and Hedong, Grand Tutor, and Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, and Prince of Fenyang, respectfully sends the official Fu Tao from the Imperial Court to inform the Hall of the Deceased Queen of Loyalty and Integrity: May her virtue shine like the sky, her brightness equal the moon, her model motherhood for all nations, her influence reaching all palaces, her bright support for the sage, her assistance in the education of women, her name recorded in history, her praise spread in songs and poems. Suddenly, she departed from the world and ascended to the spiritual realm, causing grief and anger far and near, and the palace mourns and misses her deeply. I am fortunate to be among the relatives, my son is married to a princess, her beauty of heaven and earth, nurtured and cherished, our family and relatives are honored, all the clan members respect her. Now the mausoleum ceremony is complete, the ancestral carriage arrives on time, but I am limited by the duties of a regional governor and cannot accompany and serve at the imperial palace, look forward to the spirit carriage, and cannot bear the grief and longing. I am deeply grateful for the imperial favor, graciously treated as a royal relative, allowed to perform the sacrificial rites, exceeding the common people, unprecedented in ancient and modern times, only created by the sage, extremely fearful and grateful. Respectfully offer the sacrificial food and wine. May the spirit enjoy the offering.

Emperor Dezong, recognizing the high rank of De Guo (De Guo) and his connections through marriage to the imperial family, often referred to him as a minister without using his name. Whenever imperial messengers and palace attendants visited, they would inquire about the joys and sorrows within his household. Two of De Guo's beloved concubines, who competed for his favor and status, would argue over their public and private contributions to his success, becoming resentful and refusing to face each other, which De Guo could not prevent. The emperor learned of this and bestowed gifts of gold, silk, and jewelry, ordering palace attendants to bring wine to reconcile them. As they drank, he commanded singers to perform to accompany the wine. However, one concubine, still angry, pulled out a reed instrument and placed the wine cup before the seat, saying, "No need for singing when the wine is finished." De Guo Ai, a son of De Guo, had a strained relationship with Princess Shengping. One day, he scolded her, saying, "Relying on your father being the emperor, is that it? My father regrets not being the emperor." The princess, angered and weeping, rushed to the palace to report the incident. The emperor responded, "You do not understand; his father indeed regrets not being the emperor. If he did not regret it, would the empire belong to your family?" He then wept and simply ordered the princess to return. De Guo Ai was detained by De Guo, who went to the palace to await punishment. The emperor summoned him and comforted him, saying, "A saying goes: 'If one is not deaf or dumb, one cannot be a good father-in-law or mother-in-law.' The words of children and women in the inner chambers, why should ministers listen to such things?" He bestowed gifts and sent him away. De Guo struck De Guo Ai dozens of times and then let him go.

Emperor Dezong ascended the throne and highly valued ritual and law. During the mourning period, he invited the Prince of Han to eat a simple dish of mallow leaves without seasoning or dairy products. The emperor's aunt, who was widowed, would visit the palace during festivals. If her attire was slightly extravagant, the emperor became very displeased. On another occasion, when she dressed appropriately, the emperor showed greater respect. When Emperor Dezong first ascended the Hall of Diligent Governance, the people were unaware of it. They saw a man dressed in green, riding a donkey and wearing a hat, stop and look up at the hall for a long time before continuing eastward. The emperor immediately ordered the capital's governor to search for the person based on his description. The governor called upon the official responsible for capturing criminals, Li Rong, to expedite the search. Li Rong stood thinking and confidently stated that he would find the person. Upon leaving, he gathered all the officials and artists from various departments within several miles of Chunming Gate and searched them thoroughly. The person dressed in green was indeed among them. When questioned, the person replied, "I was a musician from the Imperial Music Bureau during the Tianbao era. During the reign of the previous emperor, I often visited this place. Each time I came, crows would gather on the building, calling it the 'old crow following the carriage.' Since I moved to live outside the city, I no longer saw this. Now, seeing many crows gather again, I felt as if the past had returned, and I was moved to tears." This information was reported to the emperor. The emperor ordered that all such musicians be collected and returned to the Imperial Music Bureau. Li Rong was promoted by the capital's governor and later became a prefect.

Emperor Dezong once hunted in the imperial garden during late autumn. On that day, the weather was slightly chilly, and the emperor said to his close attendants, "The clothes worn in September and the robes in February do not match the season; I wish to advance the timing by one month. What do you think?" All around him bowed and expressed their gratitude. The next day, he ordered the Hanlin Academy to discuss the matter, and then issued an edict. Li Jifu, who was then the chief of the Hanlin Academy, submitted a memorial stating that the emperor could follow the heavenly timing and fulfill the principles of nature, and requested that the change be announced to the world and included in the regulations. Premier Li Cheng, who was initially a scholar, refused to sign the memorial. He submitted a formal report saying, "I respectfully follow the *Lüli* (Rites of Zhou), which states that fur coats begin in October. The *Lüli* was edited by Emperor Xuanzong and should not be changed." The emperor then stopped the proposal. From this point on, he did not get along with Li Jifu.

Emperor Dezong personally attended to all state affairs, paying close attention to appointments both inside and outside the court. My uncle, who served as an inspector in the eastern part of Zhejiang, was specially appointed as the magistrate of Gao陵 County. Counselor Pei Wǔ, also served as an inspector in Fufang and became the magistrate of Yòuyáng. Both were appointed under the same decree. Several days later, while touring the imperial gardens, an official approached and the emperor asked, "Where are you from?" The official replied, "I am from Gao陵." The emperor said, "You are from Gao陵, and I have recently chosen a good magistrate for you. Do you know?" In the early years of Emperor Xianzong, Liu Zongyuan and Liu Yuxi were summoned to the capital. Soon after, Liu Zongyuan was appointed as the governor of Liuzhou, while Liu Yuxi was appointed as the governor of Baozhou. Liu Zongyuan requested to be transferred from Liuzhou because Liu Yuxi needed to serve his parents, and Baozhou was considered the worst place. The emperor did not agree. The chancellor said, "Liu Yuxi has elderly parents." The emperor responded, "I only want to give him a bad prefecture, why should it matter if his mother is still alive?" Counselor Pei Jin said, "Your Majesty is serving the Empress Dowager, it is inappropriate to say such things." The emperor looked embarrassed. Later, he told those around him, "Counselor Pei truly cares for me." Liu Yuxi was then reassigned to Liánzhou. Emperor Xianzong knew Quan Wengong very well. Later, Quan Changru, who was in charge of the salt and iron administration in Fujian, accumulated a large amount of corruption. The authorities reported this to the emperor, who said, "This must be punished to the fullest extent." Counselor Cui Xianguan interceded, saying, "He is a relative of Deyu." The emperor replied, "Deyu must not have had a son who committed embezzlement; if Deyu himself had committed embezzlement, I would not pardon him, let alone his relatives?" When it was known that his mother was old, he was spared execution, but flogged one hundred strokes and exiled to Kangzhou for life.

Emperor Wenzong was about to conduct an offering at the southern suburbs, and before the ritual, the responsible office presented wrestlers. The emperor said, “I am currently in a state of purification—how can I be watching this?” His attendants replied, “This has always been the old custom; they are already waiting respectfully outside.” The emperor said, “This must be for the purpose of giving out rewards—let them go and wrestle outside.” He immediately gave them gifts and sent them away. He also awarded a prize for watching a cockfight. The entertainers praised it as “a great rooster,” and the emperor said, “Since the rooster is good, I will give it to you.” Emperor Wenzong granted the Hanlin scholar-officials ceremonial robes. Later, there were those who were waiting to be summoned and who wished to be granted rewards first to their own offices, so they submitted their names. The emperor said, “A gentleman and a petty person should not be rewarded on the same day—wait for another day.” Emperor Wenzong wished to appoint Wei Wen of Xuanzhou as a Hanlin scholar. Wei, however, earnestly declined, citing his late father’s last command. Later, the emperor said to the officials who were next in audience, “Wei Wen—every time I have wished to use him, he has refused. Then, why should I use Wei Wen at all?” His voice and expression were both severe. Cui Lü, the Minister of Revenue, said, “Wei Wen is only following his father’s command.” The emperor replied, “Wei Wen’s father did not allow his son to serve in the Hanlin Academy—that is an unreasonable command. How can that be called proper?” Cui said, “If a son can follow a reasonable command, that is already the highest form of filial piety. How much more so if he can follow an unreasonable command without changing his course? This is especially commendable, and Your Majesty should not be surprised.” The emperor said, “Indeed.” He then ceased the appointment.

During the reign of Emperor Wenzong, there was a monk who walked on the dangerous parts of the tower as if they were flat. He replaced a column at the top of the tower, and the entire city rushed to see him, all thinking he was a miracle. The emperor heard about this and said, "This tower was certainly built by human effort. Could the craftsmen of the time also be gods?" Later, the monk was punished for practicing sorcery. Emperor Wenzong spoke with the scholars of the Hanlin Academy and discussed the writings of previous dynasties. Pei Shiren repeatedly mentioned the name of Chen Shiyi. Liu Shiren looked at him, but Pei did not notice. The emperor turned to Liu and said, "His courtesy name is Boyu, so he should be called Chen Boyu." During the reign of Emperor Wuzong, Li Yanzhou once spoke in person to the emperor that the recluse Wang Gui was capable of serving as a censor. The emperor asked, "Who is Wang Gui's father?" Li replied, "He is the son of Wang Qi." The emperor said, "Generally speaking, a recluse should be a person from the countryside. Wang Gui's father is a high official. How could he live in the countryside without holding an official position?" Li had no answer. The emperor also intended to grant clothing to Du Cong's son Wu Yi. The officials listed the items, but the color of the robe had not been approved. After a while, the emperor said, "I cannot give him a white robe; he is young and has no official position, so it would be inappropriate to grant him this attire. So I will just grant him a blue robe without a shirt."

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, officials from the two main administrative bodies met with the emperor. The emperor said, "You are all my loyal and remonstrating ministers; you must each uphold justice and avoid private factions. The matters you discuss, I will certainly act upon. If you act out of personal interest, even if your advice is correct, it will be of no benefit." In the seventh year of the Dazhong era during winter, an imperial decree was issued that the following year on the first day of the first month, the emperor would preside over the court ceremony at the Hanyuan Hall. At that time, Sui Lin, who was a left remonstrator, requested that the ceremony be held at the Xuanzheng Hall instead. The next day, after submitting his remonstration, Sui heard the emperor say to the chancellor, "There is a remonstration from a remonstrating official. Should the ceremony at the Hanyuan Hall for next year be canceled?" The chancellor Wei Gong Mo replied, "The grand celebration in the first year of an emperor's reign, offering congratulations in the main hall, is a customary ritual, especially during times of peace. Your Majesty meeting with all the feudal lords, the grand rituals of the court, cannot be omitted." The emperor said, "Recently, Huazhou reported that bandits from Guanghua attacked Xiaogui County. Additionally, the Guanzhong region has been without rain or snow for a long time, both of which are my concerns. How can I say there is nothing to worry about and cancel the ceremony? If we were to temporarily hold the ceremony at the Xuanzheng Hall, what harm would that cause?" The chancellor, upon receiving the imperial decree, was just about to announce it when the astrologer reported that the sun would be eclipsed, and the ceremony was thus canceled. Later, the chancellor, during a meeting with the emperor, mentioned that there were many vacancies among the remonstrators and requested to appoint eight more. The emperor said, "The duty of the remonstrators is to fulfill their roles diligently. It is not about numbers; even three or four people like Zhang Daofu, Niu Cong, and Zhao Lin are enough! They must inform me of matters I have not yet heard."

**卷二 商部上** The merchant serves as an official, and all officials from high-ranking nobles to those with ranks are included in this department. Guo Ziyi, stationed in Fenyang, once recommended a county official, but the imperial decree did not come down. The magistrate Zhang Tan spoke to his colleagues, saying that the chancellor's refusal to approve the recommendation showed a lack of respect for Guo Ziyi's status. Hearing this, Guo Ziyi told his subordinates, "Since the difficult times, the court has been overly lenient with regional military officials, granting them whatever they asked for. This leniency has led to the regional military officials becoming arrogant, causing the court to distrust them, and resulting in this situation. Now that General Guo has recommended a subordinate who was not approved, it is probably because the request did not align with the emperor's wishes. The emperor's favor and closeness, not treating me as just a military official, allow you all to celebrate!" Those who heard this admired his fairness and loyalty. While in Hezhong, Guo Ziyi banned unauthorized horse riding, and violators were punished by death. The son of the wet nurse of Lady Nanyang was caught violating the ban and was beaten to death by the military commander. Guo Ziyi's sons wept and told him about the military commander's arbitrary actions. He scolded them and sent them away. The next day, he sighed several times in front of his guests. Everyone was puzzled, so they asked him slowly. He said, "My sons are all incompetent." He then explained, "He does not appreciate his father's military commander and is concerned about his mother's wet nurse's son, what kind of incompetent is he?"

My uncle, the Dean of the Imperial Censorate, Liu Jun, was in charge of the Fenyang military records when he had a son, bringing joy to his family. Whenever there was a grand feast in the army, the high-ranking official would instruct his attendants, saying, "Tell Master Liu that his mother is coming in the sedan chair, and we should be informed first." When Zhao's sedan chair arrived, the official descended the steps and stood with his subordinates to welcome her, then retreated to the pavilion. He once told Liu Jun, "Li Guangbi served in the military early on and could not fully care for his family. Today, I am fortunate to hold such a significant position, receiving more favor than I deserve. Although I am in a position of great honor, it does not bring me the same glory as your family." He then wept softly, unable to control his emotions. He also said, "If your mother agrees to visit Li Guangbi's home, let Lady Nanyang and others prepare the meal, and Li Guangbi will serve the food himself, ensuring everything is taken care of!" However, Zhao chose to live a simple and modest life and never visited once. Zheng Zhen, the Duke of Sima, was a high-ranking official who, whenever stationed in a regional command, honored and supported public affairs, arranging and displaying utensils with meticulous care. He never cut corners in hosting banquets and rewarding officials. In his daily life, he lived a simple and frugal lifestyle. Marriages within and outside the family were frequent, and all gifts were carefully considered. He lived alongside his cousin, the Prince of Tai, who resided in the south, representing the southern branch of the family; Zheng Zhen's residence was in the north, representing the northern branch. People referred to them as the "Southern Zheng" and the "Northern Zheng." Zheng Zhen's older brother, the Duke of Wenxian, served as a chancellor to Emperor Dezong. They were also known as the "Senior Zheng" and the "Junior Zheng."

Han Yuese Gao served as the Governor of Jingzhao Prefecture, while Wei Xiangguan, as a Prefectural Magistrate, attended to his duties. When Wei Xiangguan later became chancellor, Han Yuese Gao was appointed as the Minister of Personnel. Whenever Han Yuese Gao visited the chancellery, Wei Xiangguan treated him with special respect, showing deference to his former subordinate. Additionally, during Han Yuese Gao's time as governor, there was a prolonged drought, and a county official was tasked with reading the prayer text. The official was so focused on remembering Han Yuese Gao's family name that he mistakenly called out the name of a former chancellor. Han Yuese Gao merely frowned and ordered the text to be read again, without punishing the official. In Xia Kou, Han Yuese Gao once suffered from a minor wound and had a doctor apply ointment. The ointment was too hard, and Han Yuese Gao asked why. The doctor replied, "It's cold, and the ointment is hard." Han Yuese Gao laughed and said, "Han Gao is indeed hard." He did not make a fuss about it, demonstrating the demeanor of a great sage. Initially, Han Yuese Gao was demoted and reassigned to Qiantang, where he did not get along with Li Shuren. Later, when Han Yuese Gao was stationed in Ezhou, Li Shuren dreamed of hanging ice on a high tower and interpreted it as a sign that Han Yuese Gao would replace him, which he deeply disliked. Indeed, Han Yuese Gao was later reassigned to govern Zheyou. Since the time of his father, Han Yuese Gao's family had passed down a folding fan, which was never given lightly to servants. Upon returning home, he personally placed it on a bed in his room, to show respect and caution.

My ancestor, the illustrious Lord of Tian Shui, was appointed as the Minister of Personnel with the rank of Left Censor. His son, Du Yuanying, passed the imperial examination with distinction and was recommended for the position of Assistant Governor in the Southern Commandery. When Du Yuanying became Chancellor, the Lord of Tian Shui resumed his role as Minister of Personnel. Later, when Du Yuanying was dispatched to govern the Western Sichuan region, he recommended Song Xingxi, a former Chancellor, as his Assistant Governor. After several years, Du Yuanying was demoted to the position of Prefect of Xunzhou due to the invasion by southern barbarians and died there. Song Xingxi, as a former Chancellor, was falsely accused and exiled to assist in Kai Prefecture. After several more years, the Lord of Tian Shui passed away. He served eight terms as Minister of Personnel, governed three commanderies, all with the title of Governor and the rank of Secretary, except for the Ministry of Works, where he served twice in the Ministries of War, Personnel, and Rites. He passed away at the age of eighty-seven, having never suffered from a serious illness. His longevity and numerous honors made him the foremost figure in the central court.

Chancellor Li Yuanyong, while traveling, served as a model for scholars and officials. As a high-ranking official, he showed utmost respect to his stepmother, Lady Xue, fulfilling the duties of filial piety, and conducted himself consistently as he did when he was an ordinary person. The servants of Lady Xue, some even calling him by his pet name, treated him with familiarity. Li Yuanyong was serious and cautious, always vigilant when he was outside his home. In the presence of Lady Xue, he never displayed a stern demeanor towards his family, always gentle and respectful like a younger brother. He promoted harmony both within and outside his family, unmatched in his time. Many family members in dire straits found solace and stability through his efforts, the number being countless. While serving as a regional military governor, when his children had other matters to attend to, he ensured that his presence was discreet and unobtrusive, known to no one in the areas he passed through. His nephew, Li Yinggui, who held the position of Water Department Assistant, asked Li Yuanyong to help him buy a house, but Li refused. He privately told his close associates, "Li Yinggui advanced through his scholarly and modest conduct; just as he entered the government offices, he moved into a new house. It would have been better for him to rent a place for now." After Li Yinggui's death, Li Yuanyong took care of his orphaned children, showing them extra kindness and arranging their living situations, ensuring they all had a good reputation. Another relative, Li Li, who was ill, entrusted his children to Li Yuanyong. When Li Yuanyong was appointed as an inspector in Xiang Kou, he arranged for the marriage of Li Li's orphaned daughter, personally examining and handing over all the small and insignificant items in her possession. Li Yuanyong came from the prestigious Qinghe Cui family and later married into the Xue family. He served alongside his uncles and cousins in the same positions of regional military governors and government offices. He also served as an inspector alongside his step-uncle, and his father-in-law, Yan Pushe, held high-ranking positions. Despite his high status, Li Yuanyong never considered himself superior to others, always showing the utmost respect to those of lower rank, as evidenced by his conduct.

Quan Wen Gong De Yu, though not rising through the imperial examination, presided over the imperial examinations for three years. His students, who succeeded him, became high officials. The flourishing of talented people was widely praised at the time. The Li family from Zhao County, descendants of three ancestors, saw one member from each branch become chancellor during the early Yuanhe era. The Southern ancestor of Fan, the Western ancestor of Ji, and the Eastern ancestor of Jiang were all third-generation descendants. During the Tahe and Kai Cheng eras, another member from each branch held the position of chancellor: De Yu, the son of Ji; Gu Yan, the second cousin of Fan; and Jue, a close relative of Jiang. Such occurrences were rare among the various clans. Li Shangshu Yi had a clan member and an illegitimate son with the same name, both descending from the title of Guzang Gong. People at the time referred to the official as "Literary Li Yi" and the illegitimate son as "Family Li Yi." The official also had a distinguished family background. Once, during a ceremonial gathering among kin, he returned home laughing and told his family, "It's very funny. Today, at the ceremonial table, the two seats are occupied by Li Yi."

Among the high-ranking officials known for their kindness to relatives and old friends, there were predecessors such as Minister Zheng of the Three Dukes, and in the intervening period, Yang, the Director of the Imperial Academy, known as Ma Liuqing Yuan, and more recently, Li Zongmin, the Chancellor of Wudu. Few among the gentry could rival them in virtue. Pei Wu, the Minister of Works, supported his widowed sister-in-law and cared for his nephews and nieces, earning praise from his in-laws and extended family. After his death, Lady Cui, the wife of the Minister of Works, would weep whenever she spoke of his virtue. The Minister of Works was named Ji, a man of noble character and the eldest brother of Wu. Both brothers held positions among the eight highest officials of the court. From Chancellor Yaoqing to Ji’s son, Tai Zhang of the Ministry of Works, four generations of their family served in the northern and southern provinces. Among their extended family members who held prominent positions, there were too many to count. Jing’an Li, the Master of the Imperial Academy, though holding a high rank, did not let his authority isolate him from others. During banquets with his attendants, he would converse and laugh freely, expressing the sentiments of a commoner without pretense, and never held grudges over mistakes. He was skilled in drinking. In the summer months, by the water, he would use lotus leaves as cups, filling them to the brim and tying them securely before bringing them to the lips of those nearby, piercing them with a bamboo tube. If the drink was not finished, he would drink again. After the banquet, someone remarked that yesterday’s drinking had been very joyful. The official replied, “If we speak of today’s joy, then yesterday’s unhappiness must also be mentioned. Regardless of whether it was good or bad, it cannot be spoken of.” Duan Wenchang, the Chancellor, was narrow-minded in character. At banquets, if a guest made even a minor mistake, he would certainly express surprise or disapproval. While in the Western Circuit, there was a scholar-official named Xue Taibai who, during a drinking session, was called by name too often. The next day, he was never summoned again.

Li Fengji, the Grand Tutor, was in charge of the imperial examination. The list of successful candidates was completed but not yet announced when he became a chancellor. The Minister of Rites Wang Shangshu took over to announce the results. The successful candidates then met their master at the Ministry of State Affairs, an event that was ironically referred to as "students who follow in the footsteps of their master," a practice never seen before in previous eras. Liu Qizhou, the Governor of Guilin, served as the Chief Magistrate of the capital city. He enforced strict and clear orders, punishing without sparing the powerful. Prior to this, the capital's lower social classes, including butchers, wine sellers, and merchants, were often affiliated with various military units and did not obey the laws of the prefecture or county. They oppressed the gentry and plundered the poor and weak, and when they committed crimes, they would escape into the military, making it impossible to pursue them. Under Liu's administration, all such individuals were thoroughly punished. Some even hid in the military and falsely claimed to be commoners. Within a short period, the cunning thieves and habitual criminals in the city were intimidated and ceased their activities. I once accompanied a friend into the market, where a military man, drunk, accidentally bumped into my friend's donkey. The surrounding young men shouted, "What a foolish man! He dares to approach a scholar?" Everyone seemed to have a Duan Yi (Liu Qizhou) on their head, trembling with fear and unwilling to commit any wrongdoing. However, when speaking to his subordinates, Liu never lost his temper, nor did he scold any official. People often said that officials should focus on their own duties. On fine days with beautiful scenery, they could freely enjoy themselves without being restricted.

The Governor of Xuanwu, Li Si, in his leisure moments away from military affairs, found pleasure in playing the lute and reading books. He made his own lute by gathering old and new pieces of paulownia wood, cutting and gluing together those that sounded in harmony with the musical scale, and discarding the rest. Thus, he possessed two exceptional lutes, known as "Resonant Spring" and "Harmonious Bell." He did not like the sound of the lute and zither, but he did enjoy the performances of his two favorite concubines, Xiu Nu and Qi Qi, who were both clever and skilled in playing the lute, zither, and singing. He also wrote his own lute score. His son, Yuan Wai Lang of the Ministry of War, was the son of the Governor of Xuanwu. Although he was related to the son of the chancellor, he possessed an elegant and profound demeanor, a cool and detached personality, and excellent moral character. He also had the spirit of the mountains and forests. His skills in the art of the lute, the virtue of wine, and the style of poetry were all exceptionally high. He never approached women, loved to meet and engage with people, and did not like vulgar conversation. In the morning, he wore a headscarf and dressed in simple clothes, and when entertaining guests, he was always in a state of contentment, thus passing the day. He often collected ancient artifacts, and in Huzhou, he once obtained a piece of ancient iron that produced a clear and resonant sound when struck. He also kept a monkey named "Mountain Master" and would sometimes take it with him. On moonlit nights, he would sail on the river and climb Mount Jinsan, striking the iron and playing the lute, with the monkey always responding with its call. He would drink until dawn, without waiting for guests. He was with the father of Lin in the governor's office in Zhe West, living in close proximity, admiring the father's conduct and poetic style, and sharing the deepest bond. The wife of his uncle, a high-ranking official, Lady Wei, was the aunt's younger sister, thus connecting the two families.

Although I did not have the chance to see him, I often heard from my elders about his style and appearance, and they described him as truly like a celestial being. I also heard that Lady Xu, the wife of the Marquis of Qian, although she had two sons, experienced minor disagreements with her husband in middle age. However, when the subject was with his mother later, their affection deepened. Lady Xu's character and disposition became even better than before. Having chosen him among her sons, she loved him greatly, with a difference that was like heaven and earth. The heavenly decree of demotion, indeed, is not false. The salary he received while in office, he entrusted to his younger brothers, without asking about the amount, and he only provided for the two widowed aunts, Lady Cui and Lady Yuan, with great respect and courtesy. Lady Yuan had virtuous conduct, and the Sacrificial Master of Hongnong has already written about her, so I will not repeat it here. When the subject first arrived in Jinling, while sitting with the head of the household, Lord Qi, he often praised the beauty of Zhaoyin Temple. One day, Lord Qi held a banquet at the temple. The next day, he said to the subject, "You often praised Zhaoyin Temple. Yesterday, during the banquet, I looked at it closely, and it was no different from the state." The subject laughed and said, "What I admire is its wild and rustic charm. If the distant mountains were covered with green silk, the ancient pines wrapped in colorful objects, the stench of cooked meat polluted the spring, and music disturbed the sounds of the birds in the mountains, then it would indeed be inferior to the hall of my uncle." Lord Qi laughed heartily. By nature, he only loved tea and could brew it himself. He told people, "Tea must be roasted over a gentle flame and brewed with a lively fire." A lively fire refers to the flame of charcoal. To the point where he did not limit the number of cups and held the tea utensils all day without tiring. He once served as an envoy and traveled to the east of Xiasi County in San Prefecture, loving the clear stream, and forgot to leave for ten days.

Zhang Hongjing held the position of chancellor for three consecutive generations, a feat unprecedented in previous dynasties. Yang Juyuan wrote a poem in praise of Zhang, saying: "Yizhi's ancestors were unknown, and Wei Xian's descendants were not as distinguished." At the time, it was said that Zhang could speak of the Zhang family's lineage. Yang Juyuan, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong, composed poetry without using new or fashionable language, focusing on practical and grounded forms, and he had deep expertise. He recited poetry from dawn till dusk without stopping. Pei Ji served as a high-ranking official in the Tang court and passed by the Ministry of Personnel during the selection of officials. He told his fellow officials: "We have had many lucky breaks. These people should be given a slight promotion, but what is there to ask about? Everything is predetermined, and nothing was limited." Pei Ji did not believe in fortune-telling or divination and did not like taking elixirs. He often said to others: "Chicken, pig, fish, and garlic, eat whatever you come across. Birth, aging, sickness, and death, when they come, they come." His character was broad and open, and he exemplified such qualities. Shen Lishibu, a high-ranking official in the Tang court, had a nature that was neither fickle nor artificial. He treated things with harmony. He observed three regions, all of which were fertile lands, but left them with nothing saved. His residence in the capital was narrow and humble, and he did not add even one beam. The officials he recruited were all famous scholars. After his death, his family became very poor, and his two sons continued his career, both gaining fame. Their good deeds were not in vain. Zhang Hongjing's father, who served as an external official of the Ministry of Rites, wrote the "Jianzhong Chronicle." The style was concise and clear, and even Song, Han, Fan, and Pei could not surpass it. After this, there was no equal. Zhang continued his father's role as a historian and, when appointed governor of Hunan and Jiangxi, was ordered to compile the "Xianzong Chronicle" while in office. He was honored at the time.

Duanru was known for his filial piety towards his parents. His parents had a mental condition where they would become upset, and every time they beat someone until blood was drawn, they would be happy for a day. Duanru once received a beating while dressed in his official robes, but he never changed his expression. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong, he was officially honored and praised for his filial conduct. Another example is Li Daoxu of Zhao County, whose late wife, Lu, was known for her strict nature. She also exhibited similar behavior. Li Daoxu was already famous for his reputation and held a high position, but often when guests arrived at his door, they found him being beaten. When Liu Yuan first became the Governor of Jingzhao and was on his way to the government office, a low-ranking officer of the Shence Army rode a horse without yielding to him. Liu beat the officer to death in the street. When he reported this to the emperor, Emperor Xianzong sternly questioned him about his unauthorized killing. Liu replied, "The Governor of Jingzhao is the model for the entire country. I was just appointed by Your Majesty. A low-ranking officer of the Shence Army rode a horse and rushed past me, showing disrespect to Your Majesty's laws and not just insulting me. I beat an impolite person, not a Shence Army officer." The emperor asked, "Why didn't you report it to me?" Liu said, "I should have decided to punish him, not reported it." The emperor asked, "If he had died, who should have reported it?" Liu replied, "If it happened in the street, the street commissioner of the Jinwu General should report it; if it happened in a neighborhood, the left and right patrol commissioners should report it." The emperor then stopped questioning him. Volume Three, Section on Commerce and Business.

Han Yu and Meng Jiao were close friends. Han Yu's writing was of the highest caliber, while Meng Jiao excelled in five-character verse. At the time, they were collectively known as "Meng's poetry and Han's prose." During the Yuanhe period, later generations followed Han Yu as a model, leading to a significant change in literary style. Liu Zongyuan, Li Ao, Huai Su, Feng Ding, Yang Gong, and my mentor Li Gong were all highly respected by students for their literary achievements. Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Huai Su, and Li Gong all dedicated themselves to guiding and mentoring young scholars. Yang Gong was particularly skilled at encouraging good deeds. Whenever he found a good phrase, he would repeat it all day, and people considered this habit a quirk, but he remained true to his original intent. Since the Changqing period, Li Gan's writing was very refined, and he promoted others with a public spirit, much like the others. Li Gan was a disciple of Li Weidu, and people at the time considered him a valuable addition. Unfortunately, Li Gan's fate was unfortunate, and he did not have the opportunity to serve in a position of selection and evaluation. Since the Yuanhe period, those skilled in both literature and calligraphy included Liu Zongyuan, Liu Gong, and Yang Gong. Liu Gong and Yang Gong were particularly skilled in composing short poems. Zhang Jie was skilled in composing long narrative poems, and Li He was skilled in composing new-style music poetry. At the time, people who wrote long narrative poems followed Zhang Jie and Li He. Li Cheng, Wang Qi, Bai Juyi, and Zhang Zongsu were the best in the field of literary composition. Their brothers and close relatives continued the family tradition of historical writing, and the Su family had the largest collection of books, with no other families able to compare. They were all highly respected by later generations for their high moral standards and elegant demeanor. I myself passed the imperial examinations and succeeded at the same time as my older brother, setting a beautiful example in the scholarly community.

Cheng Zi Qi Xi of Guangping, before he took the Jinshi examination, wrote a three-volume work titled *Cheng Zi Zhong Mo*. Upon reading it, Han Yu, the renowned scholar, highly praised it. When Cheng Zi Qi Xi later took the examination, he told the examiners, "Cheng Zi Qi Xi should not be ranked below the other candidates." At that time, he failed the examination, but he made a strong appeal for justice. Later, when Hu Shangshu Zheng was in charge of the examination, Cheng Zi Qi Xi finally passed and was appointed as a test official, serving in the army of Jingyuan. When Li Fengji, the Grand Tutor, was in a high position, he saw Cheng Zi Qi Xi's work and specially recommended him for the position of Left Censor. Ultimately, due to Li Fengji's influence, Cheng Zi Qi Xi perished in obscurity. He lived a life of integrity and hardship, engaging in pure and pleasant discussions about good deeds, and many scholars supported him. It is regrettable that he did not achieve a higher position. He had the deepest understanding and the thickest friendship with his maternal uncle, Li Xinzhou Yu, and Pei Gong Yi Zhi, all of whom were respected figures in the scholarly community. Hu Shangshu Zheng was from Hedong. During the reign of the Prince of Tian Shui, who governed Hedong, Hu Shangshu Zheng established a military commission and went to Zhenwu, observing the traditional courtesy of a son visiting his parents' hometown. He held a visiting card and introduced himself as a commoner. He presented a poem to the governor, saying, "Poetry and books enter the capital, and flags pass through the hometown." The local people were proud of him. My nephew, Cheng Lu, when taking the Jinshi examination, wrote an article titled *Local Register*, greatly praising the prosperity of Hedong's people, all of which are true records. Among his fellow townspeople, the Zhao family was the most prominent, known for their scholars and officials. His great-grandfather and grandfather were both in charge of drafting imperial edicts. Cheng Lu and his four brothers all passed the Jinshi examination and served in the central government. Lu Shao Fu Hongxuan, Lu Shangshu Jiandici, Hongzheng, and Jianqiu were all his nephews, and they were known as the "Zhao family's offspring." The Jing family, the author's mother's family, also originated from Hedong, and their reputation and status were considered very high. Cheng Lu wrote about them in his *Local Register*.

During the time when Yang Yuqing served as the head of the imperial examinations, he passed Li Shiji in the exams. Later, when Yang Yuqing's son, Li Sifu, became the head of the examinations, a gathering of his students awaited him, and Li Shiji was present among them. At that time, people referred to the relationship between the Yang family and Li Shiji as "upper and lower students." Li Shiji, who was known as Li Shang, was a student of Gu Shangshu, the imperial secretary. Within a few years, Li Shiji was appointed to command the Hui-Bo army. Due to his successful performance in reporting to the court, he was specially granted a purple robe, while Gu Shangshu still wore a red robe. People at the time referred to this as "Li Shang, the imperial secretary, wearing a purple robe beneath the red robe of his mentor." Li Wudu Gong, another high-ranking official, was also in charge of the imperial examinations. His students were mostly talented and promising, including Tang Chong, Xue Xiang, and Yuan Du. They were collectively known as the "Young Bamboo Shoots." During the Yuanhe period, the calligraphy style of Liu Zongyuan, known as "Liu of Liuzhou," became widely popular among younger generations. Particularly, his expertise in the running script style was highly valued and treasured. Even in the southern regions of Lake and Xiang, children learned to write in his style, and many became proficient. Since the Changqing period, Li Gongquan, the imperial secretary, was renowned for his extensive knowledge, strong memory, and skill in calligraphy. He remained close to the emperor and was often in his service. The Liu family had two prominent calligraphers in recent times: Li Shang and Li Gongquan. They served together in the Hanlin Academy during the Kai Cheng period and were known as the "Great Liu Shashi" and the "Young Liu Shashi," respectively. The Liu family had a long tradition of scholarly excellence, with generations of members serving in high-ranking positions. Li Shang, despite facing initial setbacks in his career, eventually achieved great success. He topped the list of successful candidates in the imperial examinations and later became a high-ranking official. He was known for his integrity and honesty, which earned him the respect and loyalty of many scholars and officials.

In the third year of Kai Cheng, I was humbly ranked among the successful candidates. The examiner, Langya of the Ministry of Justice named Hekan, was a disciple of Chancellor Cui Qun. On the day he was ranked, he met his chief examiner in a small hall at Chancellor Cui's new residence. Before becoming an examiner, he temporarily stayed at the former residence of the chancellor and also met his disciples in this hall. That year, eight candidates were ranked, and six of them subsequently advanced to official positions. I, being of humble background, only became an official late in life. The top-ranked candidate, Sun from Henan, was a county magistrate before me. After Pei Shufang pacified the Huai West region, Emperor Xianzong bestowed a jade belt upon him. As Hekan was on his deathbed, he returned the jade belt and asked his disciples to write a memorial, but none of them were satisfactory. He then had his disciples take out paper and pen, and dictated the memorial himself, saying: "The treasure from the imperial treasury, bestowed by the previous emperor. I dare not take it to the afterlife, nor should I leave it in the world. I respectfully return and present it sealed." Those who heard of it praised his concise and orderly manner.

The Duke of Jin, during the reign of Emperor Dezong (Zhenyuan era), composed the essay "The Assignment on Forging Swords and Halberds into Farming Tools." The opening lines read: "In the thirteenth year since the Emperor ascended the throne, the world is as clear as a mirror, and every corner is leveled. All within the realm are driven to focus on farming, showing the world that there will be no more warfare." Emperor Xianzong pacified long-standing enemies and frequently achieved peace and prosperity, particularly in the 13th year of the Yuanhe era. The Duke of Jin, a scholar-official, became chancellor and eventually established remarkable achievements, serving as an assistant to Emperor Xianzong. Observing the style and grandeur of his writings, how could there not be extraordinary deeds in the future? The candidate for the imperial examinations, Li Wei, composed the essay "The Assignment on Tears" and four other essays titled "Light," "Thin," "Dark," and "Small." Li He wrote the *Yuefu* collection, often focusing on themes of flowers, grass, and butterflies. Yet, these two individuals did not achieve great things. Literary works can determine the quality of one's destiny. Duke Linghu Chu, a high-ranking official, was summoned from Heyang and arrived at Wenxiang, where a strong wind destroyed the house and killed the official horses that a subordinate officer was feeding at an inn. Upon arriving in the capital, he was soon promoted to a high position. At that time, Wei Yitong was serving as a temporary chief of staff, in charge of three cities. The subordinate officer, fearing punishment from the commander due to the death of the horses, wrote a report asking for a signature to confirm it. The official wrote a judgment: "In the state of Lu, when stables were burned, the sage teacher was only concerned about harming people; in Wenxiang, when the house collapsed, the chief of staff should not be concerned about the horses."

Gu Zhuo from Xinye, during the early years of the Zhenyuan era, served as a military officer in Henan Prefecture. He had an elderly sister at home. At that time, the prices of goods in Luoyang were soaring, making it difficult to provide food. Gu Zhuo often stopped his own meals to offer them to his sister. Initially, his colleagues mocked him greatly, but later, when they learned about his actions, they all praised and sighed. Gu Zhuo lived in Jianxiu. Zheng Huangu from Xingyang, young and talented, loved learning and was naturally filial and friendly. When he first lived in Qingzhou, he encountered Li Shidao gradually obstructing imperial orders. He supported his elderly parents and returned to Luoyang. He and his younger brother carried a sedan chair, rushing back and forth every morning and evening, and both of their shoulders were injured. His wife, Liu, was the daughter of the former chancellor Yuan and fully observed the duties of a wife. His brother Qigu loved gambling and betting money. Huangu's household treasures, even those belonging to his wife, were allowed to be used freely, and Qigu spent it all immediately. Whenever Huangu went out, he sealed the keys and entrusted them to family members, saying, "Keep it for the twenty-ninth son to repay gambling debts, do not let others fall into debt and be deceived by bad people." His brother was moved by his intention and began to moderate his behavior. His cousin, who wandered and loved playing the reed flute, joined the army in Xuchang as a soldier. Huangu sent someone to call him, washed and bathed him personally, and slept on the same bed with him. He then wrote letters to the governors he knew, asking for other positions. Relatives and friends valued him for this, but he was rigid and impetuous, liked to hold opinions, and did not fit in with the times, which is a pity.

Minister Li Xuanzuo, originally from Kuangcheng in Huazhou, once passed through his hometown while on a military campaign. He wished to show respect to the local officials, but they firmly declined, leaving him disappointed for a long time. Prior to this, he had prepared several baskets of gold and silk to gift to the local officials, but he stopped when he realized they were too timid. Although Li Xuanzuo rose to the rank of chancellor, his mother continued to weave one bolt of silk every month, symbolizing her humility and diligence. Whenever Li Xuanzuo handled official duties, he would see the local officials bowing to him, and upon returning home, he would tell his mother, "I see the officials bowing to me with great respect, and I feel a sense of unease. I recall my father, who served as an official in this county, often trembling in fear before higher officials. Now, I sit at the head of the table and await their reports—how can I feel at ease?" He then reminded himself of the heavy responsibility placed on him by the imperial court, urging him to be willing to sacrifice himself. Thus, Li Xuanzuo remained loyal throughout his career. At that time, many relatives and old acquaintances from his hometown, due to their proximity, sought his favor. Li Xuanzuo did not want to promote them to the ranks of military officers, nor did he want to place them among the low-ranking soldiers. Instead, he appointed them all as low-ranking officials known as *jiangpan guan*. These positions typically came with a red robe and a silver fish badge, outwardly signifying honor, but in reality, they were often idle and unimportant. As the number of such requests grew, eventually, someone submitted a petition complaining about the situation. One part of the petition read, "When a raspberry falls to the ground, it turns red; when a young lamb makes a sound, it is useless." Upon reading this, Li Xuanzuo laughed and reassigned them to other positions.

Prince Lu Wenxue Hongjian, named Yu, had ancestors whose origins were unknown. A monk from Longxing Temple in Jingling, surnamed Lu, found a newborn baby on a dike, took care of it, and thus adopted the surname Lu. When he grew up, he was intelligent and talented, well-versed in learning and eloquent, witty and skilled in debate, comparable to Dongfang Manqian. He had a deep and close friendship with my maternal grandfather, the Magistrate of the Household Department. My maternal grandfather had a letter of recommendation, written by Mr. Lu. He had a fondness for tea and was the first to create the method of boiling tea. Today, in households that sell tea, they make clay figurines of him and place them among the tea utensils, saying they bring good fortune in tea. I still remember meeting an old monk from Fuzhou who was a disciple of the monk Lu. He often recited this song: "I do not envy golden jars, white jade cups, morning entry into the provincial office, or evening entry into the imperial court. A thousand times I envy the waters of the Xi River, which once flowed down to Jingling City." There are also many poems that mourn and commemorate the monk Lu.

Lady Cui, the wife of Cui the Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel, was the daughter of the Duke of Xiping. On the Duke's birthday, there was a grand banquet in the central hall. During the meal, a young maid whispered to Lady Cui for a long time, and she nodded and left. Shortly after, she returned, and the Duke asked, "What was the matter?" She replied, "Your highness was slightly unwell last night, and someone went to inquire." The Duke angrily threw down his chopsticks and said, "It is a great misfortune that I have such a daughter! As a wife, how can you neglect to check on your mother-in-law's condition and not bring medicine, while attending a birthday celebration for your father? If I have such a daughter, what is the point of celebrating my birthday?" He immediately sent the sedan chair back and went to Lady Cui's home to inquire about her illness, apologizing and thanking her for the lack of proper instruction in raising children. All relatives and friends heard about it and were both ashamed and moved. Thus, Lady Li's virtues as a wife were fully developed, and she managed her household with strict discipline. Neither the noble nor the common people were allowed to follow fashionable hairstyles and clothing. Among the families of meritorious officials, the Xiping family was especially noted for its propriety and etiquette. Pei Xia served as the Recorder of the Ministry of Personnel in Shaanxi. Li Qian Gong Mian was appointed as the Chief Secretary and Inspector. Upon his arrival at the office, he had all the officials pay their respects. He then ordered Pei Xia to be called separately and sat down to speak with him, making a face-to-face agreement: "In a short while, there will be a banquet. Please attend together with the Chief Secretary." When the banquet began, Pei Xia was summoned three times but did not come. Li Qian Gong Mian was extremely angry. The next morning, he called Pei Xia and rebuked him, saying, "I am honored to serve as your official superior, and I have heard of your name. Moreover, we are new friends in court, and I have spoken about your good deeds. I thought we could meet informally. Therefore, I went beyond the usual etiquette and personally invited you to the banquet, but you did not come despite repeated summonses."

How could you treat me so carelessly?" Hua said sternly, "The Censor-in-Chief should reflect carefully. I do not know who has erred, but it is necessary to correct names and roles, each taking charge of their own domain, as the ancients taught. I dare not forget this. In your office, there are already guests and officials, I am merely a low-ranking clerk, how could I be treated equally?" The Prince of Qian said, "I have indeed been mistaken. Please return to your place." After Hua withdrew, the Prince of Qian immediately ordered his carriage to visit him, bowed and asked to be seated at the guest table. Hua's son, Chong, was a junior official in the Temple of the Supreme Ancestor, very young. At the time, high-ranking officials in the capital who were responsible for evaluating the performance of others and were considered respected, by custom, should receive the highest evaluation. Chong's peers, by custom, only received a middle evaluation. They complained to the head of the department, saying, "This is an old custom." Chong said, "The Minister of Rites holds a high and important position, different from other departments. The Deputy Minister of Rites is in a place of public scrutiny, and his actions should set an example. The purpose of the evaluation system is to reward diligence, so evaluations should not be tied to rank. If evaluations were based solely on rank, then the Minister of Rites should receive an upper-middle evaluation, the Deputy Minister of Rites should receive a middle-high evaluation, the head of the department should receive a middle-high evaluation, the clerk should receive a middle evaluation, the music official should receive a low evaluation, and we would deserve to be beaten!" The head of the department laughed and felt ashamed, and thus specially gave Chong an upper evaluation.

Cui Qun, the prime minister, served as the governor of Huazhou. Lu Zhen, the magistrate of Zheng County, requested leave because his name sounded similar to that of Cui Gong, which was considered disrespectful. After inspecting his office, Cui Gong questioned his subordinates and was surprised that Lu Zhen was absent. His attendants explained that Lu Zhen had requested leave to avoid the name. Cui Gong said, "The position of county magistrate is an official appointment and should not be avoided privately by the governor, leading to neglect of duties." He summoned Lu Zhen and ordered him to return to his post. Lu Zhen submitted a petition asking to temporarily change his name to Tian. Cui Gong approved the request and warned him, saying, "In public settings, it is permissible to use a temporary name, but official records should retain the original name, as there is no record of Lu Tian in the central government." Thus, Cui Gong demonstrated his understanding of the broader principles. Liu Yuan Gong was friendly with Zhang Zheng Fu, who held the position of Shangshu (Minister). Liu Yuan Gong's son, Zhong Ying, met Zhang on the road and dismounted to bow, but Zhang could not stop him. On another day, Zhang spoke to Liu Yuan Gong about the incident, saying that Zhong Ying was too humble when he met Zhang. Liu Yuan Gong responded angrily and silently, and Zhang stood up and left. Liu Yuan Gong sarcastically remarked, "Zhang Zheng Fu and Gong Chuo are close friends; does he want his son to ride a horse and collide with Gong Chuo? This person is not worth talking to." Zhang heard the remark and bowed to apologize. While serving as an official in Western Sichuan, Liu Yuan Gong had taken a concubine, and his colleagues knew about it. Some colleagues repeatedly asked him to bring out the concubine, and Liu Yuan Gong responded, "A man should have one wife and one concubine to manage household affairs and clean the house. Gong Chuo bought a concubine, not a prostitute."

Lu Zhongyuan from Fanmo lived in Anfeng of Shouzhou. His wife was Cui, a niece of Rategeng Ling Qian, from Qinghe. Cui's brother had a small plot of land, about one hundred acres, east of Luoyang. He worked diligently in the fields to sustain himself and never sought help from others. One day, while tilling the soil, he found a bottle of gold, totaling one hundred taels, and kept it secret, burying it inside his home. On his deathbed, his wife Li was worried about their poverty and the young children, fearing they would freeze and starve after his death. Cui sent away everyone and told his wife about the buried gold, pointing out where it was hidden. He instructed her, "Be careful not to tell anyone. When Lu Langzhong comes, you can tell him." Shortly after, Lu went on a journey and passed through Luoyang, paying his respects to Cui's orphan. Li sent a servant to convey a message: "I have a sad and urgent matter and must meet my brother-in-law in person." Lu agreed to see her. When they met, she recounted her late husband's wishes. Lu wept for a long time and said, "Only as my elder sister commands." Li then secretly sent a trustworthy messenger to deliver the gold to Lu. Lu then resigned from his post and traveled to Yangzhou to sell the gold. At that time, gold was expensive, and he received eight thousand coins for two taels. He bought southern goods and brought them back to Luoyang, providing land and housing for Cui's orphan and managing the family affairs. After completing these tasks, he returned home. It was only after more than a year that he resumed his official duties. Throughout this, he never told anyone about it, and only close relatives knew about it. Lu Junsheng, also known as Ziyan, had a clear reputation and was highly respected, becoming a famous minister of the world, indeed a reward for his hidden virtue.

There was a reader of the *Xiao Collection* who asked about the descendants of the *Gongcao* and whether he had any. I replied: "Emperor Wu of the Liang dynasty, the founding emperor, had a father named Shun, whose biography is recorded in *The Book of Qi*. Emperor Wu accepted the throne through abdication and honored Emperor Wen. The third son of Emperor Wen, named Hui, was enfeoffed as Prince of Poyang and posthumously honored with the title 'Zhonglie'. Hui's son, named Xun, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Yifeng. Xun's son, named Zao, served as Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince of Tang. Zao's son, named Su, served as General of Wuwei. Su's son, named Shan Yi, served as the Governor of Yazhou. Shan Yi's son, named Yuan Gong, served as a Military Aide in the Left Guard. Yuan Gong's son, named Min, served as the Magistrate of Mì County. Min's son, named Ying Shi, served as the *Gongcao* of Yangfu and was posthumously honored with the title 'Wen Yuan Master'. Master had one son named Cun, whose courtesy name was Bocheng. He served as an Assistant Minister of Finance and was honest and straightforward, possessing the virtues of the *Gongcao*. At that time, Pei Yanling served as the Minister of Finance, relying on favors and being deceitful and cunning, and did not get along with Zhang Pang. The Ministry of Finance disliked Pei Yanling's character, and he resigned and returned to Mount Lu, enjoying himself with mountains and waters. Those who understood him highly regarded him. He died as an Inspector of the Grain Department and had three sons, all of whom died young without descendants. Only his second son, Dong, survived and served in the southern part of the county, having two sons who are now wandering in the rivers and lakes and serving as temporary officials in the prefectures and counties. The *Gongcao* married his son to a disciple named Liu Dan, whose courtesy name was Zhongyong, and he was my maternal grandfather. When Han Wengong was young, he often received recognition from Xiao Jinfu."

When he entered the position of Grand Master of the Imperial College from Yizhou, he passed through Jiangzhou on his way, and thus visited Mount Lu, stopping at his residence in Jinbu Mountain. Upon learning that all his sons had passed away, he found only two daughters left. He then composed a poem saying: "The Middle Rang has a daughter who can carry on the family business, Bo Dao has no son to manage the household. Today, passing by my old retreat in Mount Kuang, I only shed tears of grief against the mist and clouds." He left a hundred bolts of silk to help them. It was rumored that Gongcao was summoned by Li Linfu, at a time when he was in mourning. He paid a visit, but Li Linfu looked down on him and did not rehire him. Xiao then wrote the "Essay on Felling the Cherry Tree" to criticize. This is probably a false accusation by those who opposed him. Gongcao's filial piety and love were well-known among the literati; Li, the Minister of Personnel, praised him for risking danger to bury his parents. How could there be any violation of propriety? This matter is below the level of what Xiao Gong and others would do. I have heard from the elders of my clan that Li Linfu heard of Gongcao's name and wanted to meet him, knowing he was in mourning. Later, hearing that the mourning period was over, he ordered someone close to Gongcao to convey his intentions, asking for a meeting at a temple near Xiao's residence. Gongcao agreed. Li Linfu left the Zhongshu Province and went to the temple, thinking of his own high status as a chancellor, and expecting that Gongcao would come to meet him where he got off his horse. But Gongcao was already inside the temple, weeping in anticipation. Li Linfu had no choice but to go forward and pay his respects. He became angry at Gongcao for showing off his talent and treating the chancellor with disrespect, and ultimately did not question him further. Later, I met the current chancellor Cui Gong Xuan, who said the same thing. Cui Gong's maternal grandmother, Lady Liu, is also my cousin, and she is the granddaughter of Li Beihai. Lady Liu is intelligent and has a strong memory, and she also gained recognition from her own clan. She can be considered a reliable record.

My examiner, the Lord of Longxi, served as a vice-minister and recommended Kong Wen, the Shangshu, and Xu Shang, the Chancellor, to serve as high-ranking censors. When Kong Wen became a vice-minister, the Lord of Longxi was detained abroad for many years and was later appointed as a junior official in charge of the royal family's affairs before returning to court. Meanwhile, Kong Wen and Xu Shang both served as Chancellors at the same time, and every time they attended banquets, contemporaries considered it a grand event. One can also sigh at the nature of official careers.

During the sixth year of the Taihe era, Te, a high-ranking official of the Tang Dynasty, served as the magistrate of Weinan and was responsible for the examination of candidates for the jinshi degree at the Yizhou Prefecture. At that time, Du Cong, a chancellor, was the governor of the capital region. He intended to ask a close friend to inquire about the results and invite Te to visit, offering him tea and wine. When the friend mentioned the candidates to Te, Te hurried down the steps, bowed down, and did not respond. Du Cong finally dared not speak and stopped. That year, nearly thirty people were in the top category, and all passed within a few years without any failures. No one before or after could match this success. Quan Shizifan served as the Inspector of the Dianzhong Shiyushi. One day, a low-ranking official was caught seeking something from the market and was punished with ten to fifteen strokes on the buttocks. Another day, another official was caught doing the same thing and was reported to the head of the central government, who punished him with fifteen strokes on the back. His colleagues were confused about the different punishments. Quan explained, "The first official was seeking something from the left military unit. The authority of the central government has been weak for a long time, and many officials still do not obey. How much more so, then, for someone relying on the power of the imperial guards! The first official received a lighter punishment because he was known to suppress the powerful and could be given a reduced penalty. The second official relied on the power of the central government to intimidate the common people, so even a minimal punishment was too light." Zhang Jiefu, who had served as an official in Xiangzhou, arrived in the capital first. Many of his friends and relatives sought financial help from him. Some of his colleagues were angry or ridiculed him and refused to sign a document. Quan was the only one to sign it first and said to the others, "I am not very familiar with Zhang Jun, but I heard that he was in dire straits and had lost his horse, and he was at a time when he was seeking a position and knowledge. It would be inappropriate to let him walk. Moreover, what is the significance of a thousand coins? It is inappropriate to recommend someone I do not know well, and in fact, I did not follow the crowd in signing the document."

The Wife of Yuan Pei, Criminal Ministry Censor, and Her Descendants The wife of Yuan Pei, a censor in the Criminal Ministry, was the sister of Quan Bai. She was virtuous and well-versed in literature, and she wrote a treatise titled *Nüyi* (Ladies' Conduct), also known as *Zhixun* (Direct Teachings). After her husband's death, she observed widowhood and followed the teachings of the Taoist deity Xuan Yuan. She received the Taoist talisman from Master Wu Jun and lived a life of intense devotion, achieving longevity. Her eldest son, Gu, was well-known early on and held various high-ranking positions, including provincial official, governor, and vice president of the Imperial Academy. Her second son, Cha, passed the imperial examination and served in various administrative roles before retiring to Mount Lu. Cha's eldest son, Lin, was devoted to the Dao but did not seek official positions. Cha's second son, Chong, also passed the imperial examination and was devoted to the spiritual and mystical aspects of Taoism. Volume 4, Section on "Jiao" (Horn) (Those who do not seek official positions are included in this section) In the early years of the Yuanhe era, the Taoist priests Tian Liangyi and Jiang Hanhong of Mount Heng (Nanyue) were renowned for their exceptionally high spiritual attainments. They were respected by people far and near and were known as Tian and Jiang. Tian had a mindset focused on the Taoist concept of *wu* (nothingness), treated all things with kindness and warmth, and did not engage in superficial or ostentatious behavior. His character was clear and lofty, and those who met him had all their narrow-mindedness and greed dispelled. Lü Wei and Yang Ping, both officials holding the rank of *Shilang* (Attendant), successively served as governors of Hunan Province and treated Tian as their teacher. During a severe drought in Tan Prefecture, prayers for rain were unsuccessful, and someone suggested inviting the master. Yang Gong remarked, "Is Master Tian someone who prays for rain for others?" Reluctantly, he was invited. The master arrived in the prefectural capital with unkempt hair and ragged clothing, happily going by carriage. He remained silent and, that very day, it rained. At his temple on Mount Heng, an inner yellow talismanic altar was set up, and the ritual implements were already arranged. The sky was overcast and dark. His disciples asked him to pray for clear weather, but he also remained silent, sitting with his headband tilted and hair hanging down. When he began the ritual, his attendants helped him adjust his hat and shoes and helped him ascend the altar. Immediately, the sky cleared up.

Once there was an old woman from the village who brought a fine blue silk tunic to present to the Master. Before the gathered crowd, the Master put it on immediately. Those present secretly laughed, but the Master took no offense. Once, Yang the official invited the Master to Tan Prefecture. At that time, the Master was just washing his feet when the messenger arrived. Without waiting, the Master boarded a small boat and set off immediately. The attendants, carrying his shoes and socks, caught up with him at the government office. The Master then sat on the brick steps outside and put on his socks, as if no one were present. Yang bowed twice in respect, but did not stop him. The Master loved to drink, yet never spoke of auspicious or inauspicious matters, or of right and wrong. When Yang was demoted from the capital prefect to a county magistrate, he sent a messenger to visit the Master, along with a gift of silverware. The Master accepted it and immediately gave it all to his disciples to use for a Buddhist ceremony. When the messenger returned, the Master said, “Tell your master, I will return soon—do not worry.” Not long after, Yang was indeed transferred to become the chief official of Hangzhou. Liangyi never sought to ingratiate himself with others; when people came to visit, he did not refuse them, and he did not remember their official positions, names, or ranks. His bond with Lu Wei was the deepest. Later, when Lu Wen became the prefect of Hengzhou, he came to visit him, and the attendants first informed him that the official was the son of a former minister.

When Wen arrived, he got off the bed and patted his back, saying, "Are you the son of Lu Wei?" Wen, moved to tears, descended the steps, and Tian did not stop either. Such was his true simplicity. Liang Yi's mother was a nun at Xiwang Temple, and the nuns all called him "Xiaoshi" (Junior Master). He would often carry two bundles of firewood to serve his mother, and if there was an obstacle preventing him from going, his disciples would deliver it on his behalf. It was said that a nun at the temple saw a tiger outside the door of Lady Tian in the morning and ran to tell her. Lady Tian said, "This should be Xiaoshi sending firewood; there is no need to fear it." Jiang Jun possessed a mixed and primal energy, though not as great as Tian's, but his cultivation and interests were also similar. He treated Tian as an elder brother and was called the closest of friends. Jiang Jun initially excelled in divination and magic, hiding his ways, and no one knew about it. Later, he lived at Jiuzhen Temple, and once he sent his disciples to the county to buy food. They did not return on time, and he explained the reason, saying, "They saw a fierce beast blocking the path at the mountain pass, and it stayed for a long time, so they were delayed."

Jiang said, "I have been protecting it for a long time, how could I dare to do such a thing?" He immediately placed a talisman at the place where he had seen the beast, and the next day, the beast fell dead at the talisman's feet. Jiang heard of this and said, "I originally used the talisman to drive it away, to make it not come, but I did not know it could not free itself. Since it has already harmed something, what use is the skill?" He took the talisman and burned it, and from then on, he no longer paid attention to it. There was a man named Ouyang Ping, whose industry was also high, and he treated Jiang Jun like a brother, and was nearly a disciple of Tian Jun. Ouyang once dreamed of three golden braziers descending from the sky, as if summoning him. After waking up, he secretly told others, "The two masters will not be long for this world, I will follow them." Soon, Tian Jun passed away, followed by Jiang, and then Ouyang also died. Chen Guanyan, Xu Lingfu, and Feng Yunyi, three men from Tongbai Mountain, were all disciples of Tian. Zhou Hunchun from Mount Heng was a disciple of Jiang. Chen and Xu were in the southeast, ranked alongside Tian and Jiang, while Feng was in the same category as Ouyang. Zhou entered Daoism since childhood, with clear and strict methods, and is now the leading figure of the Southern Mountain. The Daoist Tao Tianhuo was from Annam. He lived near the sea, and when the sea overflowed, his family fled in fear to avoid the water. When Tao was born, his mother could not take him away, and the entire family mourned. When the water receded and they returned, the infant was found safe on the intersecting branches of a mulberry tree, unharmed, and he cried and nursed as usual. Thus, he was named 'Tianhuo.' As he grew up, he was clever and straightforward, with abundant inner vitality. From the Yuanhe to the Dahan period, he served as a high-ranking Daoist priest, and was admired and respected by both the court and the common people.

The southern region has many renowned monks. Since the reigns of Emperor Dezong (779-805) and Emperor Xianzong (805-820), Yuezhou has monks named Qingjiang and Qingzhou, while Wuzhou has monks named Qianjun and Qianfu. They were called the 'Two Qings of Kuaiji' and the 'Two Qians of Dongyang.' A monk from Wuxing named Zhuo, with the courtesy name Jiao Ran, was skilled in composing regulated verse. He once visited Wei Suozhou, fearing that his poetic style might not align with his. So he wrote several dozen ancient-style poems on a boat as a gift. Wei Suozhou did not praise them at all, and Zhuo was extremely disappointed. The next day, he wrote his old works and presented them. Wei Suozhou recited them and greatly praised them. He then told Zhuo, 'You almost lost your reputation. Why not just present what you are good at, instead of trying to please me?' Zhuo greatly admired his keen discernment. Since the reign of Emperor Xianzong (805-820), various monks and Taoist priests in the capital, especially numerous were those with the title of 'Great Virtue.' Sometimes they gained positions due to favorable circumstances, thus taking it as a title.
