Each based their discussions on their respective professions, taking their own religious practices to match the title of 'Great Virtue.' This is still somewhat reasonable. There were even those who called themselves 'Great Virtue in Literature.' The title of 'literary excellence' was not meant for Buddhist monks. This is a great error. It is similar to when Emperor Dezong sought out talented individuals who did not seek fame. Someone in Zhaoying County met a scholar who rushed to the capital, asking what he was seeking. The scholar replied, 'I am going to take the examination for those who do not seek fame.' Could such an examination exist? Such titles deceive the ignorant, and all such cases are like this.

During the Northern Qi dynasty, Yang Xiuzhi was granted the title of king, but he said, "I am neither a slave nor a barbarian, so why am I being made a king? This would certainly be a disgrace for scholars like Lin Yuan and Tao Yuanming." There was a Buddhist monk named Wen Shu who gathered crowds to spread corrupt and vulgar teachings, pretending to speak about the scriptures. The wicked people spread and promoted these teachings, and the common people, especially the less educated, enjoyed hearing them. The temples showed great respect and veneration for Wen Shu, calling him "Buddhist monk." The music hall imitated his tone and style, turning it into songs. The common people were easily swayed, but Buddhist monks who understood the true teachings and those with a deeper understanding of the text were greatly ashamed of it. Recently, a monk in charge of religious duties, who did not fear the authorities and looked down on the educated class, was the most extreme case. He was beaten and exiled to the frontier several times. During the Yuanhe period, there was a monk named Jianxu who was a novice with no moral conduct. After he was executed for a crime, later people created a method of cooking meat attributed to him, which became popular. Other monks could have done it as well, but it was attributed to Jianxu. Similarly, the *Treatise on Talent and Destiny* attributed to Zhang Yan, the *Biography of Han Yu*, the *Song of the Old Ox* attributed to Bai Juyi, and the *Poem of the Buddha's Relics* attributed to Zheng Sishu were all fabrications by later people. Therefore, the language used in these fabrications is often shallow and vulgar.

Lu Ziyang recounts that in his youth, he accompanied his esteemed relative, Zheng Changshi Dongzhi, on a journey to Dangtu in Anhui Province. They lived in seclusion in a mountain cave, which was the site where Tao Zhenbai practiced alchemy. The traces of the furnace still exist, and the place later became a Buddhist temple. There was a monk who was highly virtuous and pure. A well-meaning person told about his former teacher, named Yanfan, with the surname Liu. Although he was a Buddhist monk, he had studied Confucianism early on, and the locals called him Liu Jiujing. Notable figures such as Yan Zhenqing, Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Mu Zhenning, and Dugu Changzhou were all his friends, and dozens of students studied under him. At the age of eighty, he was still vigorous, and his monastic conduct was without flaw. He had a strong liking for alcohol but never drank to the point of disorder. When students brought wine, he welcomed them and accepted it. After drinking a few cups, he became more vigorous in his teachings. He had a small garden where he planted tea, which was damaged by deer. People advised him to build a wall to protect the garden, and many scholars were happy to help build the wall together. Mu Bingbu Zhi served him with great diligence. Once, he obtained good wine and secretly placed it in a small porcelain bottle in his robe. While building the wall, he told his teacher, "I have a little good wine; will the monk drink some?" Yanfan smiled and drank it. When he was slightly tipsy, he said to Mu, "There's no need to move the stones; come and listen to a lecture instead." He then analyzed profound and subtle teachings with him, never tiring of it.

Zheng further inquired about past events, and the monk sighed for a long time before saying, "Recently, there is respect for Confucianism and the Way, but none of the spirit of the predecessors." He then produced a piece of paper, which was a letter from Mu, the Minister of War, to the monk. The letter was filled with the utmost humility and respect in the form of casual conversation. It read: "I occasionally humbly accept a prestigious official position, all due to good guidance. Placing myself in the order of officials, I spend my days on trivial matters, yet I often think of the past. In past years, I was near a clear stream, shaded by tall pines, receiving service under your seat, and hearing subtle teachings. I do not know when this matter will again be achieved, like looking at the moon in the water or clouds on the mountain, merely rushing and climbing in thought. The monk has a light taste for flavors but a deep appreciation for the virtue of wine, eating like an infant and drinking like a young adult. Holding his body, he feels uneasy, and at midnight, I think about it and truly hold worry and longing." Its sincerity is like this. Under the date, it merely states the disciple's name, presenting a report before the monk's dharma seat, without mentioning the official position. At that time, to be fond of learning and serving a teacher can be said to be to the utmost. It also mentions that there is an elder monk named Zongchi, who is a friend of Yan Fan. He is clean and frugal, a lifetime without ever interfering with others. He only cultivates mountains himself, farming several acres to provide clothing and food. Sometimes encountering a bountiful year with much wheat, beside there are leftover ears of grain, estimating and knowing that its owner certainly will not collect, picking it up and bringing it home. If it is worth saving, then seek and accumulate it, call the owner to give it to them, if they do not come, then place it at the road side. Its solitary conduct is like this.

In the year of Yuanhe, the fifteenth year of Emperor Xianzong's reign, a subordinate general named Tan Keze from Huainan was captured by the Tibetans while guarding the border. Upon arriving in Tibetan territory, the Tibetans were unaware that Emperor Xianzong had passed away, and they feared the Tang army would reclaim the Hexi Corridor, causing them great anxiety and sleepless nights. After Tan Keze was captured, he was shackled and placed in a dungeon, deprived of food and water, and subjected to repeated interrogations. Tan Keze fully informed them of Emperor Xianzong's death, but the Tibetans did not believe him. There was a literate person nearby, and Tan Keze briefly recorded the imperial edict and showed it to them, and they then believed him. According to Tibetan law, wooden seals were used as official stamps, and for urgent matters, messengers would ride horses to the headquarters of a Tibetan military commander, traveling hundreds of miles in a day. The messengers would ride their horses as fast as possible, and they were called "horse messengers." The messengers reported the news of Tan Keze's confirmation of Emperor Xianzong's death. Previously, whenever the Tibetans captured Han Chinese who had no special skills, they were put to work in various roles and branded on their faces. Those with some level of cultural or literary skills had their arms marked, waiting for the order of the Tibetan ruler.

A Chinese person who was appointed as an official was called a "舍人" (a low-ranking official). He could read and write, which qualified him as a "知汉书舍人" (an official who knew the Han dynasty's records), but he did not wish to hold that position. There was an old "舍人" named Cui, who was originally a Chinese person, and the subject had met him in Lingwu. Cui was highly trusted by the "蕃帅" (a foreign military leader) and was able to intercede on behalf of the subject, resulting in his release. The subject attempted to escape multiple times but was always caught by guards or scouts. The "蕃帅" did not kill him but instead beat him with a leather whip hundreds of times, and eventually, the subject managed to escape. He spent six years in captivity and, upon returning, went to the imperial court to report his experiences. The emperor ordered him to be sent to the "神策军" (a military force) as a messenger. Before he could be promoted, he was demoted due to a military purge and was assigned to the "浙东" (Zhe Dong region) as a low-ranking officer. In the fourth year of the Cheng era, the subject met someone in Yuezhou (a place name) who walked slowly and had a scar on his arm. The person explained that he had been beaten and frozen in captivity, and the scar read "天子家臣" (a servant of the emperor). The subject also spoke in detail about the possibility of reclaiming the "河湟" (a region) and that his speech still had a slight foreign accent.

附录：谐戏** 玄宗问黄幡绰：“为什么这个孩子这么招人喜欢！”黄幡绰答道：“因为他是我自己的孩子。”玄宗又曾登上苑北楼，远眺渭水，看见一个醉汉躺在水边。玄宗问左右侍从：“这是什么人？”左右侍从不知，打算派人去询问。黄幡绰说：“他是一位满年资的令史。”玄宗问：“你怎么知道的？”黄幡绰答道：“他再过一次转正就能升官了。”玄宗听后笑了，不再追究。 玄宗又与各位亲王共餐，宁王对着皇帝的座位喷了一口饭，直接喷到了皇帝的脸上。玄宗问：“宁哥为什么喷饭？”黄幡绰说：“这不是喷饭，是打喷嚏。” 周愿曾奉命出使魏州，节度使田季安与他并辔而行。路上遇到一匹非常肥壮的驴，田季安指着驴对周愿说：“这头驴是大王世充的。”周愿应声答道：“这头驴是小窦建德的。” 李尚书选性情严厉，不喜欢开玩笑。当时周愿担任江西盐铁留后，即将到任。李公告诫属下：“周生喜欢开玩笑，有时会僭越无礼，希望各位稍微庄重些对待他。”等到周愿到任后，李公多次设宴款待。除了寒暄，李公很少与周愿交谈，周愿也没有机会发话。有一天，李公宴请亲朋好友，周愿也参加了。李公有一位老朋友的子弟前来投奔，此人无所事事。李公问及这位子弟过去住过的别墅，以及家中有技能的仆人和有名气的图书，这位子弟都说已经卖掉了。

Li Zhe said, "Since you are not yet an official and your family is poor, you have sold your property, but why would you sell your books and ancient paintings as well?" He felt sad for a long time. Then he asked, "Do you still have a copy of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) written by Yu Yongxing?" The person felt ashamed and afraid, and did not dare to admit that he had sold it. He said, "I have temporarily pawned it for money." Zou Wanyuan suddenly said, "The *Shangshu* is a great burden." Li Zhe forgot about his earlier refusal to engage in witty conversation, and asked, "What burden is the *Shangshu*?" Zou Wanyuan replied, "It has already been pawned once for the *Yao Dian* and *Shun Dian*, and now again by this young man." Li Zhe's anger grew, and he no longer resisted Zou Wanyuan. The next day, a man from a county under the jurisdiction of Hong gave birth to a child with three heads. Li Zhe saw the report and was displeased, and remained unhappy for a long time. Zou Wanyuan said, "Wait until he grows up, and let him try on a hat." Li Shu, the Censor-in-Chief, enjoyed witty banter and wore luxurious clothes. Once, after returning from court, he entered the market with his colleagues. A peddler shouted at him without yielding. Li Shu cursed, "A worthless soldier worth only a few coins, how dare you collide with an official?" The peddler turned around and said, "A scholar worth only eight coins, acting so important." Li Shu enjoyed hearing unusual sayings, and had his servant lure the peddler to his home, where he set up a feast. He slowly asked the meaning of "eight coins." The peddler replied, "It just means that my clothes are seven inches too short." Li Shu's colleagues thought this was a clever answer, and Li Shu felt very ashamed.

After passing the imperial examinations, Pang, the magistrate of Jingzhao, was appointed to serve in Shouchun. There was a candidate from the Jianghuai region named Yan, whose name was mistakenly written in reverse in the *Dengkeji* (Record of Successful Candidates). He then rented a boat and begged for food. When he visited the official, there was only one judge in the prefecture, who did not ask about his surname and welcomed him warmly, inviting him to the hall and introducing himself as a distant relative. Pang had few relatives, and he was very pleased to see the candidate, welcoming him warmly and entertaining him with great courtesy, even inviting him to eat together. After some time, when they talked about family matters, it turned out that the candidate was not related to Pang. Pang was surprised and asked, "What is your name, young master?" The candidate replied, "I am surnamed Yan." Pang clapped his hands and laughed, saying, "You are mistaken! I am surnamed Pang. What business do I have with you?" He bowed and dismissed the candidate, who still bowed and thanked him as an uncle before leaving gracefully. Yao Xian was known for his literary talent and his fondness for humor and wit, often making jokes when the opportunity arose. Yao Nanzhong, a high-ranking official, was inspecting the area around Shaanxi. When Yao Xian first paid a formal visit, he was warmly received in the main hall due to their long-standing family ties. After the formal greeting, no other topics were discussed. The area around Shaanxi was on the route between the two capitals, and visitors could come at any time. Suddenly, a visitor introduced himself as Li Guoteng. Yao Nanzhong said, "The name Guoteng is quite new; I don't know which family he belongs to." His attendants and those around him all said they did not know. He then asked Yao Xian, "Do you know him?" Yao Xian initially lowered his head and frowned, but soon could not hold back his laughter and said, "I think it might be Li Quci." Yao Nanzhong eventually realized the joke and laughed.

Lu Shangshu Hongxuan, along with his younger brother, Lu Jianci, the prefect of Quzhou, were both in the capital. One day, when Jianci was about to leave early, Shangshu asked, "Have there been any promotions or changes?" Jianci replied, "There are no major promotions or changes, only Pi Xiat叔 was appointed as the governor of Shu." Shangshu did not know that Pi was Xiat叔's surname and thought he was a relative, so he lowered his head for a long time and said, "I don't understand what you mean; I can't find Lu Pi Xiat叔." Jianci then explained, and they all laughed heartily. It is said that Li Huan was appointed as the military governor of Jin Prefecture, and his cousin, Wu Gong, was eccentric and loved the Dao. He also collected ancient objects. On Li Huan's birthday, Wu Gong did not send any gifts, but instead sent a box containing an old black robe and said, "This is the robe that Li Linggong wore when he recaptured the capital. I hope your achievements will be as great as those of Xiping." Li Huan thanked him in a letter. Later, when Wu Gong had his birthday, he sent a box containing an old, greasy cap and said, "Knowing that you deeply admire high virtue and purity, I have obtained a cap that the Mr. Hongya wore when he first became an immortal. I hope you achieve immortality just like Mr. Hongya." All the guests and officials laughed heartily. I once read Xie Chaozong's "Collected Oddities," which states: "King Yigong of Jiangxia loved ancient objects and often asked court officials for them. The Secretary of State, He Xu, had already sent him something, but the king continued to ask for more. He Xu was very unhappy. Once, while traveling on the road, he encountered an old dog collar and a worn-out apron. He then ordered his attendants to bring them back and sent them in a box. In the note he wrote: 'Knowing that you still want ancient objects, I am sending you the dog collar of Li Si and the apron of Sima Xiangru.'" This is quite similar to the cases of Li Huan and Wu Gong.

At the time of handling affairs in Quzhou, there was a woman surnamed Wang who presented a petition concerning land property, calling out, "Grandfather and Grandfather were alive." The seated guests laughed at her. Therefore, he asked for similar examples. I have personally seen: in Wangwu, there is a carpenter's daughter named Ah Jia; in the capital, there is Ah Fu; in Hongzhou, there is Ah Gu; in Shu, there is Ah Mu; in Luoyang, there are Ah Bo and Ah Lang. All of them are named after their surnames, and it is also laughable. **Volume 5: Miscellaneous Department** (The Miscellaneous Department deals with matters that are not specific to individuals or objects but are broadly discussed.) In the southeastern corner of the Shangshu Province, there is a small bridge on a major thoroughfare, which has been traditionally called the "Crooked-Neck Bridge." It is said that when the officials of the Cishi and Dianzhong bureaus, who have served for a long time, reach this point, they crane their necks to look toward the Southern Palace. On the eastern path of the southern gate of the main hall, there is an ancient槐tree whose shade extends very wide. It is said that at night, the sound of music can be heard, and within the province, there are people who can divine the future, commonly known as the "Sound Tree." The Cibu Bureau calls it the Ice Hall, saying that it is both clear and cold. In the Shangshu Province, the seals of the twenty-four departments have a tradition: they are all kept in the straight office. When the officials exchange their duties, the clerks hang them on their arms to pass them on, which is quite cumbersome. Yang Yuanzhou, serving as the Director of the Personnel Bureau, first placed them in a cabinet with a lock to store them, which people found convenient, and it has not been changed since. When the cabinet was first made, the state commander, who was then the Director of the Personnel Bureau, wrote a large inscription on it, jokingly creating a test paper: "There are thousands and tens of thousands, unable to resist laughing, pass or fail."

Inside the Secretariat, there is the Falling Star Stone, Xue Shao Bao's painted cranes, He Jian's cursive script, and Lang Yu Ling's painted phoenix — all of which are traditionally known as the "Four Wonders." During the Yuanhe period, Han Gongwu served as a secretary in the Secretariat, and he shot out one eye of a crane with a slingshot; at that time, he was humorously called the "Fifth Wonder." To the east of the Secretariat is the Right Wing Guard, a place now overgrown and in disrepair. Its main hall is so close to the Imperial Academy that it feels encroached upon, and it faces directly south toward the Censorate. Someone once mocked it with these words: "The gate is blocked by the Censorate; the temple is encroached upon by the Imperial Academy." The Censorate has three divisions. The first is the Main Office, or *Taiyuan*. Its officials are called *Shiyushi*, and the people commonly refer to them as "Duan Gong." When meeting the Chancellor or the head of the Censorate, they are addressed as "Mr. X, the Shiyushi." The official in charge of miscellaneous affairs is known as "Za Duan." When meeting the head of the Censorate, they are called "Mr. X, the Official in Charge of Miscellaneous Affairs." Even if they hold other high-ranking positions, their title as *Shiyushi* remains unchanged. When meeting the Chancellor, they are called "the Official in Charge of Miscellaneous Affairs, Mr. X, the Official." Those in the Main Office who do not hold the *Za Duan* position are commonly called "San Duan." The second division is the Hall Office, or *Dianyuan*. Its officials are called *Dianzhong Shiyushi*, and the people commonly refer to them as "Shiyushi." When meeting the Chancellor or the head of the Censorate, who is the *Za Duan*, they are called "Mr. X, the Hall Censor." The newest appointees are assigned to oversee the Right Inspection, and after some time, they move on to the Left Inspection, and are known as the "Two Inspection Officials." Their duties are complex and arduous. When they are promoted to a higher rank, they are assigned to the judicial division, where the workload becomes even heavier. Only in the middle of their careers do they find themselves in a more leisurely position. Hence, there is a saying in the Censorate: "If you are free from inspections and not yet assigned to the judicial division, you can only know yourself." This is said in a tone of wry satisfaction, indicating the pleasant ease of such a position. The hall contains a small landscape painting of exceptional skill, which is said to be the genuine work of Wu Daozi. The third division is the Inspection Office, or *Chayuan*.

The colleagues of the Inspector of Justice are called "Inspector of Justice," and people also refer to them as "Inspector of Justice." When they meet the Prime Minister and the head of the Censorate, known as "杂端," they say, "Inspector of Justice surnamed [name]." If all three departments meet the head of the Censorate together, they collectively call themselves "Inspectors of Justice from all three departments," and the clerk records their actions. At each official banquet in the hall, if the assistant is not present, there is no one to oversee, and they only bow to each other. If the assistant arrives, they then follow the strict rules of the Censorate, with the "杂端" bowing from the south and the clerk bowing from the north. The officials from the two departments sit separately, even when eating with chopsticks and spoons, they remain completely silent. After finishing the meal, the clerk holds a yellow scroll and bows, saying, "Please proceed with the business." Then the officials of the Censorate inform the "杂端," saying, "Proceed with the business." They then raise the issue, saying, "Inspector of Justice surnamed [name] has a fault, please follow the regulations." The clerk records it. If there is an error in raising the issue, the smallest official from the Palace Department raises the issue, and the smallest Inspector of Justice raises the issue. If the "杂端" laughs, then all three departments laugh, it is called "烘堂," and they are all exempt from punishment. Any penalty recorded in the yellow scroll is waived upon amnesty, and when the head of the Censorate visits the various departments, any penalties imposed on officials are also waived. Inspectors of Justice who have served in all three departments are highly esteemed, but they are weary from the monthly investigations in the Palace Department and are reluctant to transfer to the other two departments.

In the lower ranks of the judicial officials (侍御史), they are responsible for taking charge of investigations (便领推也). Many do not wish to undertake this role (多不愿为), and within the office, the phrase "from the Palace to the two courts" (以此台中以殿中转两院) has become a term of mockery (为戏谑之辞). Every day, the officials come and go (每出入行步). The lower-ranking judicial officials stand in the inner part of the building (侍御史在柱里), while the officials of the two courts stand outside (殿察两院在柱外). Sometimes, the Palace officials enter the inner part of the building (有时殿中入柱里), and they all laugh at him (共咍之曰：“著去也。”). The officials of the three courts and their clerks (三院御史主簿) report matters to the chief official (有事白端公) in his hall (就其厅). If there is an urgent matter to report along the way (若有中路白事), it is called "篸端" (谓之“篸端”), and there is a penalty for such reports (篸端有罚). The Palace officials are exempt from patrols (殿中已免巡). When the regular patrol officials are absent due to leave or illness (遇正知巡者假故), the higher-ranking officials temporarily take over (则向上人又权知), and this is called "蘸巡" (谓之“蘸巡”). When officials receive appointments or celebrate personal events (台官有亲受除拜喜庆之事), they visit the chief official, the deputy, and the head of the office (则谒院长、杂端、台长), and this is called "取贺" (谓之“取贺”). All these terms and practices are derived from the language of the clerks and runners (凡此皆因胥徒走卒之言), and thus have become established customs (遂成故事). The chief official reports to each person when he finishes his duties (院长每上堂了各报). All the judicial officials stand in the south corridor (诸御史皆立于南廊) in casual clothes and slippers (便服靸鞋) waiting for the chief official (以俟院长). When they are all standing, the chief official then comes out (立定，院长方出). They bow to each other and line up (相揖而序行). When they reach the palace court gate, they bow to the Palace officials (至殿院门，揖殿中). They line up again and bow to the judicial officials at the dining hall gate (又序行，至食堂门，揖侍御史). They bow several times when entering the hall and going to the dining area (凡入门至食，凡数揖). In general, bowing is the ancient form of respectful greeting (大抵揖者，古之肃拜也).

In the office, there is no bowing; for wine, there is no need to stand up and thank; one simply bows to the wine. Wine is the most suitable for showing respect, because standing up and thanking might be too cumbersome and disrespectful. Often, when officials from the office greet other officials, they mistakenly perform the office bow, and people laugh at them. Every time going to court, when in line at the waiting hall, there is a lying bow. At the gate, there is a horse bow. When the head of the office is in the hall, if a censor wants to go to another hall, they must first report. For punishment decisions, they must also first report. The inspection office has a general hall; when the head of the office is in this hall, all people gather to speak in the general hall, also called the southern inspection office, built initially during the Kaiyuan era by the censor Zheng Lu. The etiquette inspection hall is called the "Pine Hall," because there is an ancient pine tree to the south. The criminal inspection hall is called the "Nightmare Hall," because many people have nightmares when sleeping there. The military inspection hall usually manages the tea in the office, and the tea must be bought from Sichuan, stored in pottery to prevent heat and dampness. The censor personally seals and opens the tea, so it is called the "Tea Bottle Hall." The administrative inspection hall manages the list of people entering the court in order, so it is called the "Court Register Hall." Above the administrative inspection hall, there is the host of the guesthouse. Above the host of the guesthouse, there is the censor. The censor is the leader among peers, called the "Head of the Office." In the office, respect for the leader is important; each of the three halls has a leader. The inspection office has particularly strict rules. When censors from the three halls first pay their respects, they do not thank the court first but first visit the head of the office. If the head of the office declines to see them due to illness, they cannot thank the court or ascend to the hall.

The censor of the Northern Office, Cai Jinyi, ranked above the position of "给事中." The "中书舍人" ranked below the position of "给事中." Pei Shangshu Xiu, as a censor, was of short stature. The other officials joked with him, saying, "How can someone so short stand above us?" Pei replied humorously, "If you find it strange, just pull me down." The others all laughed. Later, he was appointed as a "中书舍人."

A scholar asked Mr. Dongjin, "Did ancient people establish laws with the intention of benefiting people?" He replied, "To benefit them." The scholar then asked, "Why did these laws later become harmful?" He responded, "Because of the benefits, evil arises, and thus they become harmful. Suiren drilled wood to create fire, enabling people to cook food and cook all things. How could one know that in later generations there would be the burning of the Qin palace in Xianyang, with a poison that did not cease for three months? Fuxi drew the eight trigrams and created written characters. How could one know that in later generations there would be the selling of fake writings, deceiving the customs of the time through market practices? Houji cultivated grains. How could one know that in later generations there would be the suffering caused by monopolizing wine, grain, tea, and salt for profit? The Yellow Emperor invented vehicles, clothing, and military equipment. How could one know that in later generations there would be the competition in luxurious and beautiful things, as well as excessive military campaigns? He also built palaces and houses. How could one know that in later generations there would be magnificent mansions and separate buildings, towering into the clouds and shining like the sun? Emperor Yao and Emperor Shun established government and ranks. How could one know that in later generations there would be the buying of ranks by paying gold or grain, falsely claiming titles of nobility? Yu the Great controlled floods and divided the world into nine provinces. How could one know that in later generations there would be taxes on mountains, forests, rivers, and lakes, as well as the difficulties of opening channels and building dams? The Duke of Zhou established marriage rituals. How could one know that in later generations there would be reliance on炫耀 relationships and alliances to seek wealth and status? Confucius edited the *Book of Songs* and *Book of Documents*, established rites and music, and taught the Five Constants. How could one know that in later generations there would be the stealing of the name of Confucian scholars, stealing fame and reputation, and falsely claiming high status? All these examples corrupt through similar means, undermining the rules and order, greatly damaging the root. Is it the fault of the sages? Is it because the sages initiated it? Is it because those who governed in later generations intensified it? Alas! The metaphor of the horse's liver, I cannot bear to speak of it.

What is to be lamented is that Yi Yin and Zhou Gong, initially acting with public loyalty, took control of the sovereign to preserve the nation and stabilize the state. However, from Wang Mang, Dong Zhuo, Cao Cao, and Sima Yi onward, those with ambitions as vast as the sea took this as a precedent, which is especially lamentable. Jie, Zhou, You, and Li, who were exiled or killed, are always taken as examples for future generations. Thus, the temporary public loyalty of Yi Yin and Zhou Gong misled later traitors and villains, while Jie, Zhou, You, and Li, though tyrannical in their time, benefited future wise rulers. A good ruler or administrator must use talents when they are available, and use them appropriately, appointing them but not favoring them, and not appointing those who lack talent. Thus, no one is wasted or overused, and there is no one in the world who laments not being recognized or harbors excessive expectations. Gao Yao said: "To know people is wisdom, and to appoint them is governance."

It is said in the *Zhuangzi*: "When employing people, he uses them according to their abilities." Zang Xi Bo said: "The downfall of a state is due to corrupt officials." Ban Gu praised Emperor Xuan: "Rewarding with certainty and punishing with severity, thoroughly examining names and realities, officials, scholars, and jurists all excel in their abilities. As for craftsmen and their tools, since the Yuan and Cheng reigns, few can match them. This is also sufficient to show that officials are competent in their duties and the people are content in their occupations." Emperor Guangwu did not allow the son of Princess Guantao to become a court official. From ancient times, wise and enlightened rulers, as well as those who achieved hegemony, and the emperors of our dynasty, who avoid involvement in minor affairs, use people according to their talents and abilities. There are many recorded in historical records. Even with the supreme authority of the ruler, living in seclusion and looking down from a height, they are still so cautious and serious about every reward and punishment for minor officials. How much more so for the ministers who assist the ruler, how could they act carelessly? When Former Qin invaded, Xie An appointed his nephew Xuan as the commander to resist them. Xishi, although he had always been at odds with Xuan, heard this and said, "An is wise to heavily rely on his relatives." "Xuan's heart does not辜负 the appointment, it is his talent." Everyone thought otherwise.

超曰：“吾尝与玄共府，见其使才，虽履屐间亦得其任。是以知之。”玄果立功于淮淝，破苻秦百万之众。郗嘉宾之言，亦可谓至公矣。今之人徒私于所亲，无安石之明；徒憎所不善，无嘉宾之论，可悲矣！李林甫奸邪阴贼，妨嫉贤能。至于善守章程，深得宰相之体。当时虽正直有名之士，多被构害。而守寻常仕进者，名获其分。至今人有以右座呼之，而不名也。非其在下有感之者乎？韩晋公节制三吴，多历年所取宾佐僚属，随其所长，无不得人。尝有故旧子弟，投之与语，更无能解。召之与宴而观之，毕席端坐不旁视，不与比坐交言。数日，署以随军令监库门。使人视之，每早入，惟端坐至夕，警察吏卒之徒，无敢滥出入者，竟获其力。

Pei Guangde, while serving in the central government, had an old friend who held a respectable official position and came to visit from afar. Pei Guangde provided him with generous support and treated him with composed and friendly hospitality, doing everything for him during his stay. The friend took the opportunity to request a local administrative position in the capital. Pei Guangde said, "You are indeed a fine man, but this position is not suitable for you. I dare not grant it because of the favor of an old friend, and destroy the rules and regulations of the court. One day, if there is a blind prime minister who pities you, it is not wrong to obtain it, but I certainly cannot." His adherence to principles was like this. Alas, when the ruler is not careful with titles and positions, it leads to treating the wise like servants; when the subjects are not careful with titles and positions, it leads to serving the ruler like infants, and weakening the court. Thus, it leads to abuse, overuse, corruption, and decay, wandering and drifting, which is worrisome, and it is uncertain whose fault it is.

Wang Binzhou Fan, who was appointed as the Right Chancellor from his position as Governor of Henan, received his official notice just as the Deputy Governor Hou Ji was hosting a banquet and invited him with a letter. Wang responded by writing, "Although the new appointment has been heard, the old title is still in place; hastily issuing an invitation is enough to be laughed at." People in Luoyang took this as a topic of conversation. According to tradition, there is a protocol difference between the Deputy Governor and the Governor during social gatherings; even if appointed, one must wait for the official decree. Someone compiled a book called *The Strange Tales* and wrote that Emperor Xuanzong ordered the Taoist monk Ye Jingneng to write talismans, which is not recorded in official history. It is not known that Ye Jingneng was executed during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong for sorcery; during Emperor Xuanzong's reign, the person who had magical abilities was named Fa Shan. Errors in conversation are still acceptable, but if they are written down, the mistakes are much more serious. Another person checked Lu Fayan's *Qieyun* and saw the phonetic characters, and said, "This is a Wu person, truly flipping characters too obscure." It is not known that Lu Fayan was from Henan, not Wu.

Another scholar, well-versed in Confucian classics but unaware of recent events, mentioned Cao Binwang and then said, "I know Cao Binwang's grandson Li Shao Fu, who has many brothers." The scholar thought that Cao Binwang was a title given to various kings. Modern people often say, "According to the decree of the crown prince." However, they do not know that the crown prince should be called "ling." Calling it "jiao" is for kings and princes. All these are errors due to a lack of understanding of the fundamentals. In human society, the right side is esteemed and considered honorable. Etiquette places guests first, so the west is where guests are seated, and the host is in the east, which is a sign of humility. Nowadays, some people seat guests in the east, which is not proper etiquette. This is because the position of responsibility is in the east, and there is a custom of playing games where the east is the south end, showing respect for those who are the leaders of a region or area. A classical text states, "The Son of Heaven does not receive guests as equals, and no one dares to treat him as a host. Therefore, when a ruler visits his ministers, he ascends the main steps and does not enter their rooms." The commentary explains, "It is not proper to entertain the ruler as a guest." Nowadays, regional governors and prefects entering their own regions eliminate the distinction between host and guest below the level of magistrates, which is still similar to the relationship between ruler and minister. As for regional commanders, who oversee subordinate counties, they are all ancient southern-facing feudal lords, but they are only acting in the capacity of officials stationed in the provinces, like those in the central government. Since they participate in banquets, they are different from the relationship between ruler and minister. However, they use the ancient ritual of the Son of Heaven ascending the main steps, which is the result of not studying and practicing the rites and thus acting presumptuously. Common people tolerate and perpetuate these errors, which is both laughable and lamentable.

In ancient times, the Three Excellencies established halls, and the prefectural governors, comparable to the ancient feudal lords, also had halls, which is why the world's titles have the term "阁下" (Ge Xia). Formerly, local officials such as the Cishi (刺史) and Taishou (太守) were also addressed as "节下" (Jie Xia). When writing to high-ranking officials like the Chancellor, they were often addressed as "执事" (Zhi Shi), meaning "your servant" or "your attendant." Liu Zixuan, as a historian, wrote to the Chancellor and addressed him as "足下" (Zu Xia). Han Yu, in his letter to the Chief Secretary, also used "执事," following the same precedent. The term "记室" (Ji Shi) originally referred to the office of a secretary or clerk in the service of a noble or official, and it was not appropriate for others to use. "执事" (Zhi Shi) specifically refers to the people around the official, and it can be used regardless of rank. "侍者" (Shi Zhe) is a term for servants or attendants, suitable for both scholars and commoners. Recently, officials up to the level of imperial censors and county magistrates are all addressed as "阁下" (Ge Xia). As for newly appointed assistants or aides, they are still called "记室" (Ji Shi). Nowadays, everyone is addressed as "阁下" (Ge Xia), which implies a lack of distinction in status. "执事" (Zhi Shi) is only used for officials, and "侍者" (Shi Zhe) is only used for monks. Now, common people address each other as "阁下" (Ge Xia), which is a result of frivolous joking and a sign of the breakdown of proper titles. Moreover, distant relatives and lower-ranking individuals often use the term "座前" (Zuo Qian), which is particularly strange. The term "阁下" (Ge Xia) is one rank below "殿下" (Dian Xia), and "座前" (Zuo Qian) is one level lower than "几前" (Ji Qian). Such improper behavior is not acceptable. However, specific familial relationships, such as uncles, aunts, and cousins, can still use these terms appropriately.

The *Book of Han* records Zhang Qian's exploration of the river's source, stating that he was sent on a distant mission but found no evidence of the Milky Way myth. However, Zhang Maoxian's *Museum of Curiosities* tells a different story, describing a recent account of someone living by the sea who, every August, saw a sea raft arrive on schedule. This person brought a year's supply of food and traveled to the Milky Way, where they encountered a woman weaving and a man tending cattle. They sent a message to Yan Junping, who interpreted it as a guest star invading the constellations of Ox and Dipper, which was believed to signify significant events. Later, it was said that someone obtained the Weaving Maid's loom-supporting stone and showed it to Yan Junping. These stories are all based on fanciful accounts. Today, there is a stone in the Yan Zhen Temple in Chengdu, commonly known as the "loom-supporting stone," which is said to have been left by Yan Junping. During the Bao Li period, I returned home after failing an examination and encountered official messengers carrying Zhang Qian's raft on the journey between the capital and Luoyang. It was originally in the eastern capital's imperial palace but is now ordered to be presented according to imperial decree, though its true nature remains unknown. Many poets of the past have used the image of Zhang Qian's raft, perpetuating errors. Even if these stories appear in various books, they are not reliable.

During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, the Secretariat was renamed the East Platform, the Chancellery the West Platform, and the Ministry of State Affairs the Wenchang Platform; hence, the Office of the Censorate came to be called the South Platform. During the reign of Wu Zetian, the Office of the Censorate bore the titles of Left and Right Censorates, and at that time was also referred to as the Left and Right Platforms. Thus, the Censorate itself had never been called the East or West Platform. Only among the common people was the capital called the West Platform and the Eastern Capital called the East Platform. When Li Qi Yun served as Chief Censor, those who did not know his name referred to him simply as the West Platform, though they did not know the origin of this custom. Could it be that because his name was "Qi Yun," they called him that? They also referred to Du Huangchang, the Grand Secretary, by his title. Should such figures be honored? How could they be discarded as if they were false deities or idols, set up without justification? If there are any discerning people, they should certainly avoid such things. Even if they were placed at great mountains, seas, rivers, famous mountains, or at the sites of emperors and sages, if they are not recorded in the classics, they are improper acts of worship. In the past, those who lived without virtue or merit, and who died without commendable conduct, were also objects of improper worship. Such figures should be cut down and burned, to warn the ignorant masses. Why, then, should one pay homage and worship them? Divine offerings, according to ritual, should be received with reverence; but other offerings from shamans can simply be waved away. Officials should take this as a warning.

A scholar returned from court and visited a friend, only to leave in displeasure upon seeing a monk or Taoist in conversation. On another day, the scholar told his friend, "Why do you associate with those who wear coarse, simple clothing? I don't know if they are wise or foolish, but I find them distasteful." The friend replied, "The distaste for their clothing is superficial. It's not as bad as the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant, which one might encounter while standing or walking alongside. You, on the other hand, seem to associate with such people without shame, yet you criticize me for befriending those who are virtuous and reclusive. In the Southern Dynasties, the wise appreciated the natural sounds of frogs in the wild over elaborate music. To me, the simplicity of such clothing is far superior to the ostentatious colors of today." Furthermore, those who lack understanding of proper principles and are tasked with writing often make serious errors. For example, the character for "official" is often written as "官," and the character for "Vesuvius" is often written as "荣." Sometimes, "荧" is mistakenly used for "髭须," and "宾" for "鬓发." Even small differences in strokes can lead to significant misunderstandings, as in the cases of "棹" and "掉," "杨" and "扬," "棒" and "捧," "傅" and "傅," "沐" and "沭," "氏" and "氐," "惟怅" and "帷帐," "赐惕" and "锡," "钅昜" and "昜." There are many such examples. Those who study writing should not consider these details trivial and neglect them.

Emperor Wuzong's temple name is "Yan," which was changed to avoid using the same character twice. The character for "言谈" (speech) has been changed to "谭" (Tan), and "淡" (light) has been changed to "曾澹" (Zeng Dan). The character for "啖" (eat) is commonly used inconsistently, and in the past, it was sometimes written as "餤." The pronunciation of "餤" is "Tan," and according to the *Er Ya* (an ancient Chinese dictionary), "餤" means "to advance" or "to progress." It is not originally a term for food. The world values "饼" (cakes) and "啖" (eating), and cooks pass down their knowledge informally. In common usage, all characters related to food use the radical "食" (food). The character "啗" has a different pronunciation. Searching through historical records, various texts, and Buddhist scriptures, it is often written as "口边敢." Its pronunciation is the same as "著两火" (two fires), which is pronounced "徒敢反" (tu gan fan). According to *Yupian* and *Qieyun* (ancient dictionaries), "啖" is the correct form, while "著两火" is a common variant, both pronounced "徒敢反." It should be written as "啖" without doubt. For "啗马" (feeding a horse), the character "啗" should be used, pronounced "徒滥反" (tu lan fan). By definition, eating something by oneself is called "啖," and feeding someone else is also called "啖." According to the *Book of Han: Annals of Emperor Gao*, it says, "Send Li Siqi and Lu Jia to persuade, and offer them benefits to entice them." The commentary by Yan Shigu says, "啗 originally means to eat, pronounced '徒敢反.' When used to feed someone, it changes to '徒滥反.' Now, it is used to entice with benefits, taking the actual meaning as the basis." Although Yan Shigu's explanation is not clear, he does not mention the character "口傍两火" (two fires on the side of the mouth). This clearly shows that the character has been passed down through common usage, and later it became a surname due to this tradition. Recently, when writing "饼啖" (cakes and eating), some people have written "食边口边作覃" (food side and mouth side as Tan) and "口边作詹" (mouth side as Zhan), which are arbitrary and very incorrect. *Qieyun* is a common reference book; why not keep it on your desk for quick reference?

Chapter 6, Feather Department The people of the south believe that large trees in dense forests are divine. They say that cutting down trees near these places will surely anger the gods and bring disaster. Someone cut down someone else's tree, and the owner complained. The officials captured and interrogated him, and it was decided that he should be treated as a thief. His family pledged their land to bribe the jailer and sold their clothes to provide food for the prisoner. Eventually, they could not escape being beaten on the back and all returned with wounds. This is avoiding disaster only to hasten it. There were also villagers who were bewitched by a neighbor's witch and believed they would have a disaster. They should thank the gods, so they killed their family's calf, brewed alcohol, and beat drums to worship. At the time, officials banned slaughtering cattle and private brewing, and the laws were very strict. Additionally, it was a national mourning period, and playing music was not allowed. They committed three crimes and were captured by the officials. The head of the household, neighbors, and protectors were all punished with severe crimes, and dozens of people were implicated. This attempt to ward off disaster actually caused disaster. Their foolishness is like this. Cao Zan, a famous actor from Hongzhou, was tall, nearly eight chi, educated, and very intelligent. He excelled in all kinds of humorous performances, exhausting all his talents. He was also skilled at water entertainment, climbing a hundred-foot mast without undressing and jumping down, sitting upright on the water as if sitting on a mat. He floated in boots on the water. Sometimes he was put into a bag by others, with the bag's mouth tied, and floated on the river, untying himself. As for circling and appearing and disappearing, changing into a thousand forms, those who saw it were terrified and amazed, and no one could understand it. There must be some other trick; otherwise, he would truly be risking his life.

The military had a performance called the "Sword Gate Passing" art. On grand feast days, they would set up tents in the courtyard for dozens of steps, arranged like corridors and halls, and swords were woven into the shape of rafters and beams. The performer would ride a small horse, carefully measure the path at the gate, adjust the direction, lower the whip, and move forward. The sound of the swords moving could be heard. After passing, both the person and the horse remained unharmed. In the Xuanwu army, a small general was skilled in this art and performed it during military feasts. The reward he received was only a few bolts of silk. One day, the overseer accidentally omitted his name, and the general became angry. He complained to the commanding general and was allowed to submit his name again. At the time of the performance, he was extremely careful in preparation. He walked several steps, but suddenly the wind rose, and the horse was startled. It touched the swords and lost balance, and both the person and the horse were killed by the swords. There was a scholar who sold his property elsewhere and received several hundred strings of coins. Fearing the difficulty of transporting the money, he asked a trusted person to deposit it in a public treasury and carried a receipt back. This is what the world calls "convenience exchange," and he placed the receipt in his clothes bag. One day, drunk, he pointed to the bag and said to someone, "Don't underestimate this bag; it contains valuable things." A thief heard this. That night, the thief killed him and took the bag, thinking it contained gold. Upon opening, he found nothing and threw the receipt into the water. The thief was caught by officials, and his actions were discovered. The slightest slip can lead to great harm; this is what is meant by being careless and causing harm. The scholar Zheng Hui said, "His home was in Gaoyou. He had a relative named Lu, whose estate was near the water. Several neighboring families, together, killed a white snake. Soon after, there was a sudden thunderstorm, causing a flood. Several families were drowned without a trace, but only Lu's family in the middle remained unharmed."

Dong Xiang Yizhi, when he was young, was named Secret. Coming from a poor family, he studied at a village school, where the teacher, an old man with magical arts, taught him. One day near the tomb, a sudden storm arrived. The students all returned home but could not, and stayed under a leaking roof. It was cold, and they competed to get close to the fire. Only Dong Gong slept on the bed, waking up at midnight. The old teacher patted him and said, "Later, Lord Secret will become a minister, reaching the highest rank and longevity, so strive to love yourself." On the day Emperor Dezong visited Fengtian, Dong Gong was just about to take the Jinshi exam and followed the emperor westward. Riding a slow donkey, he reached Kaiyuan Gate, where the road was crowded and narrow, and the gate was about to close, making it impossible to enter. He was afraid that he could not proceed. Hearing someone scold the donkey and hit it from behind, he was able to rush in quickly. Looking back, he saw a soldier in black, who called out, "Scholar, do not forget this kindness." When he rose to the imperial court, he found the soldier's son and helped him rise to high office, achieving great success in the bureaucracy. Lu Zhenbai's father, Lao Peng, had magical arts and was also known for his ability to recognize people's potential. In the early Yuanhe era, his relatives Hongxuan, Jiandici, Hongzheng, and Jianqiu all came to visit. He kept them sitting for a long time, and ordered Zhen to sit in order. He looked at them and said, "All five will become military governors, which is quite impressive!" In the end, it happened as he said. Another relative, Kai, who was just about to take the Jinshi exam, visited him at his residence in Anyi. He told Kai, "Your pursuit of fame is a great thing. But you will only achieve it in ten or more years, so do not regret the delay. You will also attain a high official position when you enter the court." Kai was promoted to the top rank in the first year of Changqing and became a Shuji (a junior official) in the tenth year of Dazhong.

Zhang Tan served as an attendant to Prince Fenyang. Once, his family experienced a strange occurrence. He summoned a diviner to inquire about it, who said that a great calamity was about to come, and only by retiring could he avoid it. Zhang Tan did not believe him. During a banquet, blood was seen on the table. A shaman heard about it and advised him to shut himself in and not receive guests, and to refrain from feasting and entertainment. Zhang Tan became angry and beat the shaman with a stick. Later, Zhang Tan's words and actions deviated from the norm, and the official bore a grudge against him. Zhang Tan also frequently mentioned matters concerning his colleagues, often waiting alone to see the official, who was often at his favorite concubine's residence. The official did not allow him to report matters and would suppress the gatekeeper to let him enter. The official considered Zhang Tan's behavior as treating a military officer with contempt and became even more resentful. Eventually, Zhang Tan asked the official to dismiss the official he had appointed, which infuriated the official. He imprisoned Zhang Tan and reported it, and Zhang Tan ultimately died from beating. Liu Zongyuan, an external scholar from Yongzhou, was summoned to the capital with the hope of being appointed to a position. One day, he visited a diviner to inquire about his fate and told him a dream: "I am of the surname Liu. Last night, I dreamed of a willow tree falling to the ground. Is this a bad omen?" The diviner said, "There is no need to worry. Just be concerned about being appointed to a distant post." When Liu Zongyuan asked for more details, the diviner explained, "When alive, it is a willow tree; when fallen, it becomes willow wood. Wood is associated with herding. Will you be appointed as the governor of Liuzhou?" Indeed, Liu Zongyuan was later appointed as the governor of Liuzhou, just as the diviner had predicted.

Li Lianggong Fengji, before he took charge of imperial edicts, had an old maid in his household who liked to talk about dreams, and many of them came true. Li had long hoped to be appointed to an official position, so he consulted the maid about it. One day, the maid came in the morning with a sad expression. Li asked her why, and she said, "Last night I had a dream about you, and it wasn't a good one. I don't want to say it." Li urged her to tell him. The maid said, "In the dream, I saw a man carrying a coffin to the back of the hall and saying, 'Let's put it here temporarily.' Soon after, it was moved into the hall. This dream doesn't seem good." Li secretly rejoiced. Soon after, he was appointed as a censor in the central secretariat. Later, he was put in charge of the imperial examination, and before he finished his duties, he entered the government. The water commissioner Cui Chuo was a young hero who did not adhere to minor details. During the Tianbao era, a Taoist magician passed by his home. Cui lavishly supported him, but had no expectations. As the magician was leaving, he left a pill as a parting gift. Cui did not value it much and buried it under the bed. During the rebellion in the Yan and Jie regions, his family fled from the bandits, but Cui stayed behind. Suddenly, he saw a fat and fresh mushroom under the bed, so he cooked and ate it, mixed with meat flavors. From then on, his body and stomach became light and healthy, and he had no more diseases. He could read small characters in the moonlight and ate raw pork at night. He was still alive at the beginning of the Yuanhe era and died at over ninety years old. He was the brother-in-law of the governor of Suzhou, and the governor's collection included a poem titled "Gift to Cui Dushui." If he had taken the magic pill properly, he would have already ascended to immortality. Secondly, if he had eaten the transformed lingzhi without mixing it with meat, he should have been able to live to a very old age. The Taoist gods only rewarded him with longevity and good health for his kindness and generosity.

When Cui Xiangguo was stationed in Xuzhou, he once cast his own divination using the *Yilin* (Book of Changes' commentary) of the Cui family. He encountered the hexagram *Qian* changing to *Dazhu*, and the divination text read: "The classic laws and legal texts are stored in the Lantai. Though they may be defiled by chaos, they alone will not suffer calamity." When the upheaval involving Wang Zhixing occurred, he was indeed appointed as the Secretary of the Imperial Archives. During the Longqing era, the people of the streets of Ezhou often added the word "niu" (牛) to their speech. There was also a monk who styled himself "Niushi" (Teacher of Cows), who was sometimes foolish and sometimes wise. If anyone offended him, he would say, "My elder brother is coming; how can you do anything to me?" Not long after, the Prime Minister, Lord Qizhang, was appointed as Chancellor and Commander of the Wuchang Army, and the phrase ceased to be used. Yet Niushi still lived. The monk was in fact the name of Lord Niu. One then realizes that the positions of general and chancellor are not accidental. Earlier, at the beginning of the Yuanhe era, Han Shangshi Gao was stationed at Xiakou and was granted the title of military governor; later, he was again appointed as the Inspector of the region. In the third year of the Longqing era, Cui Xiangguo Zhi was transferred from the Ministry of Justice to the post of Inspector. In the winter of the following year, Lord Niu indeed arrived. The prime minister was granted a military title to govern Xiakou — this was the first time since Lord Niu.

A Collection of Writings Presented to Me by Cui Zini of Boling Cui Zini of Boling presented a collection of writings to me and said: "When Liu Yi Huai was in Bian, Han Hong was the right flank guard, and Wang was the left flank guard. They were on good terms with each other. Someone accused the two of gaining military support, which would be detrimental to Liu. Liu became very angry and summoned both to interrogate them. Han was Liu's nephew, so he bowed down and smashed his head on the ground, speaking loudly for hundreds of words. Liu's anger slightly subsided. Wang was old and his legs trembled, unable to defend himself. Liu shouted and ordered him to be pulled down and beaten with thirty strokes. At that time, newly made red sticks, with heads several inches in diameter, strengthened with sinew and lacquer, could stand without falling. After five or six strokes, it should have been fatal. Han thought he would surely die. By dusk, he visited Wang's home, finding it strange that there was no crying. He thought Wang was afraid and dared not cry, so he asked the gatekeeper, who said the ambassador was fine. Han was familiar with Wang, so he went to his bedroom to ask him. Wang said, 'I have read the *Diamond Sutra* for forty years, and now it is starting to bear fruit. I remember when I was beaten, I saw a huge hand like a winnowing basket, suddenly covering my back.' So he revealed it to Han, who saw there were no marks of beating. Han used to not like Buddhism, but from this began to associate with monks, writing ten sheets every day. When he became prominent, he had written hundreds of scrolls. Later in the Office of the Secretariat, during the hot summer, a censor visited on official business. Han was sweating while writing the sutras. The censor was surprised and asked him, to which Han fully explained Wang's story. My position is in the Bureau of the Immortals, and the constant attendant Liou Gong often told me this."

Wang Zhixing, while serving as the governor of Xuzhou, enforced strict laws. One day, a guard who had just handed over his duty and was returning home, prepared a feast to celebrate with his sister-in-law. He was grinding a knife in the kitchen to prepare for cutting raw mutton. After finishing, he quickly took the knife and walked away. His sister-in-law's brother entered the kitchen from the hall, and they met unexpectedly. The blade struck him in the heart, and he died immediately. The incident was reported, and Wang Zhixing interrogated the suspect, who claimed that it was an accidental mistake and that he had no ill intent. Wang Zhixing did not believe him and ordered his execution. However, the knife repeatedly leaped out of the executioner's hand and fell to the ground, happening three times in a row. Surprised by this supernatural event, Wang Zhixing decided not to kill him. According to the *Guangling Lishi Zhuan* (Biographies of Heroes from Guangling), it is recorded: "Liu Jun, courtesy name Youjie, was appointed as the magistrate of Wanqu. Upon taking office for two years, his governance was clear and peaceful, and he was beloved by officials and people. Once, a friend visited the county, and the host prepared a feast with joy. During the feast, a dog was caught, which accidentally hit the guest and caused his death. Legal experts said, 'The host originally had murderous intent and should be held responsible.' Liu Jun replied, 'I have heard that the Prince of Chu was filial and refused to take medicine, which led to his death. The historian recorded it as a case of killing the ruler, saying, "To serve the ruler with all one's heart and strength, and to avoid taking medicine is acceptable." In this case, the host and the guest had no enmity; the host merely prepared a feast with joy and had no intention of harming anyone. Unfortunately, the guest died. According to the *Rituals of Zhou*, this should be treated as an accidental error. Why use the law to punish both parties unjustly?' The legal system, however, was rigid and would not listen. Liu Jun said, 'If there are people who lose their manners within my jurisdiction, it is my fault.' He then removed his seal and resigned." This case is similar to the one in Xuzhou, where the gods intervened to make the judgment.

The Kaiyuan Temple in Han Prefecture houses a statue of a Bodhisattva. From the top of the head to the flame-like halo and the seated posture, the entire statue is carved from a single piece of blue-green stone. Its surface is clean, smooth, and lovely, with exquisite craftsmanship, standing several feet tall. During the Kaiyuan era when many Buddhist temples were destroyed, most Buddha statues suffered damage or were broken, but this one remained unscathed. When the temple was rebuilt, the monks preserved it as an ancient treasure and placed it in the west corridor. I, along with the esteemed monk Zhi Xuan, who was in charge of religious affairs in the Western Sichuan region, and Yang Renzan, a scholar who was deeply knowledgeable about Buddhism and appreciated ancient things, visited the temple together. Yang showed deep respect throughout the day, while Zhi Xuan, with his profound mind, sharp intellect, and extensive knowledge, recounted the story: "Previously, a craftsman found this stone to be extraordinary and carved the statue with great reverence, almost forgetting to eat or sleep. A beautiful woman would regularly provide food for him. His thoughts were entirely focused on the statue, with no impure thoughts. After a long time, he became lazy and his mind wandered, and the woman then stopped coming. He suffered from hunger and thirst, and pain spread throughout his body, and he then realized that the woman was a celestial maiden. He burned incense, bowed, and deeply repented, and the woman returned, and his illness was instantly cured. The statue was then completed. There was also a record of this event, but it was lost due to the temple's destruction. The temple has now been rebuilt."

During the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (Yuanhe) and Emperor Muzong (Changqing), when people in the two capitals met in the alleys and streets, they often said, "It should be your uncle." By the time of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), the emperor favored his relatives, and several people falsely claimed to be the emperor's brother-in-law, but none of them were the true one. The saying "It should be your uncle" indeed proved true. When I was young, living by the Yangtze River, I often played with children. We made spears out of bamboo strips and decorated them with bird feathers. We tore paper into flags and made representations of battle, facing each other and saying "kill." Soon after, we stood still and said "kill again." Within a few years, Emperor Xianzong eliminated the bandits, and the powerful warlords of Cai and Qi were subsequently annihilated. The saying "kill again" came true. At the beginning of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), there were idle and frivolous people in the capital who took the names of scholars who had passed the imperial exams and compiled and arranged words according to principles of righteousness, calling it a "public announcement by scholars." In the ninth year of his reign, those who were executed were mostly scholars and literati. At the beginning of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), Wang Qian was the military governor of Jingnan. Without any reason, a white horse suddenly rushed into the government office and died, lying stiff and blocking the road. That year, Wang Qian died. This was a near miss with a horse disaster. The Jinshi Chen Cun was able to compose ancient-style poems, but he had bad luck. The examiners often wanted to pass him, but at the time, there were always reasons preventing him from passing. Xu Shang, the former prime minister, was an old acquaintance, and on the day of the exam, thousands of people wanted to appeal on his behalf. The night before the exam, he stayed at a friend's house. The friend prepared food for the exam and also breakfast, asking Chen Cun to rest and wait for the time. After the fifth watch of the night, it was strange that he did not get up. They called him while he was asleep, but he did not respond. Looking at him from the front, he was already paralyzed and could not speak.

The Jinshi Candidate Zheng Pang Zheng Pang, a jinshi candidate, had spent many years in the examination halls, yet his contemporaries had mostly risen to high positions, and he often lamented being left behind. One night, he suddenly dreamed of passing the examination and being a *tongnian* (same-year examinee) with Wei Zhoufang. At that time, the Wei family had candidates who had already passed the exams, but none bore the name Zhoufang, which made Zheng even more dejected. In the autumn of the first year of the Tahe era, Zheng went to take the examination in Luoyang. At that time, Wei Jingfang was serving as the governor of Luoyang and was a cousin of the Shangshu (Minister of the Central Secretariat). On his way to the examination, Zheng passed through Shaanxi, where the Shangshu was inspecting the border region. The Shangshu questioned Jingfang, saying: "My name is Hongjing, your brother's name is Hongfang, and your name is Jingfang. The brothers have each taken one character from my name to form theirs, which makes no sense at all." Jingfang then changed his name to Zhoufang. When Zheng heard of this, he was extremely happy and said, "Now I have hope of passing the examination!" Four years later, Zhoufang indeed became his *tongnian*. Zheng later entered the imperial court and was appointed as a *Dianzhong Shiyushi* (Imperial Censor of the Palace). The former *Xuancheng Guancha Da Fu* (Observation Governor of Xuancheng), Zheng Shuo, recounted this story. Zheng Shuo was Zheng Pang's second cousin. Zheng's son, Zi Pu, also recounted that when he was preparing for the examination, he once dreamed of looking at the list of successful candidates, and on the list, he saw only the large character "Feng" (鳳). In the first year of the Dazhong era, during winter, Zi Pu requested to be relieved of his post at Fengxiang, and by chance, he saw the first character on the list of local candidates submitted to the examination, which was also "Feng." When he arrived at the eastern capital for the examination, he heard a poem about Wang Zi Jin playing the flute on Goushan under the moonlight. All the poems around him also contained the character "Feng." The following year, he indeed passed the examination. Zi Pu was the son of Master Zheng.

In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Shen Xun, a Vice Director of the Secretariat, served as the chief examiner for the imperial examinations. His successful candidate, Li Bin, whose father was Li Cong, served as the magistrate of Wannian County. At a gathering of those who had passed the same year’s examinations and were now historiographers, Cangbu Li Langzhong Bian was present and joked with the successful candidates, saying, "Today is truly splendid, as I and my esteemed examiner are from the same year." At that time, Yousi Li Langzhong Conghui, who was also present, teased Li Bian, saying, "Not yet! I and my esteemed examiner are from the same year, how about that?" He referred to Liu Shilang, the Vice Director of the Ministry of Rites, from Chenzhou. Everyone found this unusual. On that day, several officials visited their guests. Feng Shangshu Shen, who was also present, was a fellow year-examinee of Liu Shilang, the Chancellor Yang, and they sat together, lamenting the situation. The Tutor and Advisor Zhang commented on it.

During the time when Kong Yinwen, the Yin of Henan, served as a Buque (an official responsible for correcting omissions and errors in the emperor's edicts), he advised against the campaign against the Tanguts (a reference to the Tangut people). As a result, he was demoted to the position of Sima (an official responsible for military affairs) in Chenzhou. After a long period of time, he received a letter from his elder brother, Wenye, who was a high-ranking official. The letter stated, "The Constitutional Court intends to appoint you as a Shiyushi (an official responsible for overseeing the imperial court and advising the emperor)." He eagerly awaited the imperial decree. Suddenly, he received another letter which said, "The prime minister has appointed you as the You Shi (an official responsible for recording the emperor's daily activities)." However, he did not receive any news regarding the appointment. One day, a magpie appeared in the courtyard, chirping as if speaking. His grandson, Zhi, bowed and prayed, "May I obtain an official position soon." The magpie flew away and dropped a small piece of paper, which had the words "Buque" written on it. This was considered extremely strange. Not long after, he was appointed to the position of Buque again. Kong Yinwen also mentioned that he had been known and praised by the Duke of Wudu, a high-ranking official. At that time, he served as a Sizhi (an official in the imperial court) at Dali Temple. He often lamented his stagnation in his career. Coincidentally, Zhang Mo, another official, wanted to be appointed as the Taichang Boshi (an official in the Ministry of Rites). Li Gong, a high-ranking official, said, "Zheng Sizhi has been suppressed for a long time. I will definitely recommend him to take his place." Soon after, Xiao Zan, who had completed mourning for a deceased relative, was also appointed to an official position. The other officials then appointed Xiao as the Boshi. Before this, there was a Buddhist monk named Bao Rui from Dàyún Temple, who could predict one's fortune or misfortune. Kong Yinwen asked him about his own future, and Bao Rui said, "As a Sizhi, you will eventually obtain an official position in the imperial court. However, you will first have to serve as an official in several counties. How could you obtain it immediately?" Later, he was appointed as a Shiyushi and served in Heyang, Zhe Xi, and Huainan. He was promoted to the position of Jianjiao Langzhong (a mid-level official) and finally appointed as the Bibu Yuanwailang (an official in the Ministry of Revenue). Bao Rui's predictions were not always accurate, but this particular one was extremely precise, as it involved the concept of Yin Zhi (the idea that one's actions in life can affect their fate or destiny) and the belief in predetermined outcomes.

Wang Meng was an old friend from their days as commoners, having long known Jing, a subordinate of Zhao, for his administrative ability. When Zhao became chancellor, Meng came from his previous post as magistrate of Xingan County in Ji Prefecture to pay his respects. Upon seeing Meng, Zhao was overjoyed and gave him generous support, intending to promote him to the position of censor. At that time, the number of officials in the Censorate was very low, and Emperor Dezong was very cautious about making appointments. However, since Zhao was in power and his proposals were often followed, Meng was already waiting for the appointment as a *xiu yi* official. One day, he happened to visit a Buddhist temple in Cien to consult a fortune-teller, whose name he had forgotten. Meng asked when he would be appointed to office, and the monk replied, “Looking at your face, I see no sign of good fortune. In a few years, you will be appointed to a position on the frontier as a censor.” Meng laughed heartily and returned home. A few days later, during a meeting of the prime ministers, Zhao took the opportunity to say, “The Censorate has too many vacant positions, and among them, the role of inspector is particularly important. I wish to select three or four people for the post.” Emperor Dezong replied, “It is not that I do not wish to fill these vacancies, but this position must be filled by someone upright and of proven virtue. I suppose you are only choosing young, frivolous court officials. It would be better not to appoint anyone at all.” Zhao replied, “Your Majesty’s wisdom is exactly in line with my humble opinion. I wish to seek candidates among the *lusi canjun* (officers in charge of affairs) and county magistrates.”

The emperor was greatly pleased and said, "If it is like this, it is my intention. Do you have anyone in mind?" Sui Jing then recommended two people, one of whom was Meng. The emperor said, "Bring the petition here." After Sui Jing left, he encountered Pei Yanliang, who asked him, "What did the chancellor propose that pleased you? Your face is full of joy." Sui Jing did not answer directly. Pei Yanliang left in anger, cursing, "Look at this old veteran, will what he requested be carried out?" When Sui Jing saw the emperor again, he presented his report and the emperor asked, "What did Chancellor Zhao Jing propose that pleased you?" The emperor said, "Chancellor Zhao Jing is extremely impartial." He then explained the matter of the censor. Pei Yanliang said, "This is a great mistake. Why do you trust him? As a chancellor, Zhao Jing cannot possibly know about the county officials' merits and demerits. He must have a private reason for recommending them. When he comes again, just question him about where he got the information, and you will know." On another day, the emperor asked, "Why did you know these two people?" Sui Jing said, "One is an old friend, and the other is a distant relative. I am well acquainted with them." The emperor remained silent. Later, Pei Yanliang returned, and the emperor said, "What Zhao Jing requested, indeed, as you suspected. It was thus put aside and not carried out." Meng returned to his old place, and Chancellor Zhao Jing died in his position. Several years later, the governor recommended him as an official, and he obtained the position of a temporary censor.

Chancellor Cui Shenyu, a high-ranking official, conducted investigations in the western part of Zhejiang. On his left eyelid, a growth resembling a polyp had formed, threatening to obscure the pupil and severely obstructing his vision. All previous medical treatments had been ineffective. One day, Yang Wai Mu, a judge from Huainan, acted beyond his official capacity and invited Cui Shenyu to a feast in the main hall. During the feast, Yang Wai Mu mentioned that there was a skilled ophthalmologist named Mu Zhong in Yangzhou who could treat such conditions. He suggested writing to Chancellor Cui Xuan to request Mu Zhong's assistance. Cui Shenyu agreed to this proposal. A few days later, they received a letter from Cui Xuan stating, "Mu Zhong is rather rough and may not be reliable. There is another ophthalmologist named Tan Jian who is meticulous and thorough, far superior to Mu." They then brought Tan Jian to see Cui Shenyu. Upon meeting, Tan Jian said, "This condition can be removed immediately. However, you must remain calm and undisturbed, making a decisive decision internally, and it will definitely be effective." Cui Shenyu replied, "I will ensure that, even my wife and children, will not know about this." Tan Jian added, "We need nine days of clear weather, and the treatment should take place at midday in a quiet place. If the day goes as planned, there will be no further worries." At the time, the weather was cloudy. After six or seven days, it suddenly rained heavily, and Tan Jian looked very worried. By the eighth or ninth day, the weather cleared up. Tan Jian asked Cui Shenyu, "How much can you drink?" Cui Shenyu replied, "Even if the room is small, I can still drink to the brim." Tan Jian was very pleased. Initially, Cui Shenyu had decided to use Tan Jian's medical skills and only informed the skilled physician Shen Shixiang among the military leaders, who supported the plan.

On that day, Tan Sheng was invited to the northern tower of the residence, accompanied only by Shixiang and a young page. No one else knew about it. Tan Sheng offered White several cups of wine, and they sat upright without speaking. Soon after, Tan Sheng gently touched the affected area and said, "It's a very minor matter indeed." At first, it felt like pulling it out, though it was painful, it was bearable. Then, the sound of scissors moving could be heard. White said, "This place is a bit dark; please move to the central courtyard." Shixiang and the young page helped White to the courtyard. Once seated, the sound of combing could be heard. Previously, Tan Sheng had requested several pieces of good cotton dyed red. Now, he wiped the affected area with the red cotton and applied medicine to it, and it was no longer very painful. Tan Sheng asked White to open his eyes and see the protruding flesh, which was as big as a small finger and hard as dried tendons. Then, he ordered it to be thrown into the river. Tan Sheng immediately sent someone to inform his wife and children. He then reported to Huainan, and Cui Xiangguo replied, "After sending for the doctor, I was very worried and anxious. Upon hearing that he had recovered, my mind finally calmed." A few days later, the imperial summons arrived in Jinling. Alas! If only Yang Jun had not met, if Tan Sheng had not arrived, if the official's resolve had not wavered, if it had not cleared up for nine days, if the imperial summons had come suddenly, if the deadline for returning had been pressing, because of his eye disease, his position would have been abolished. How could he hold power and assist the emperor as a trusted advisor? These several events sufficiently demonstrate divine assistance. But after he became chancellor, Tan Sheng had already passed away, how thin his fate was indeed!
《因话录》 [唐] 赵璘 卷一 宫部 玄宗柳婕妤，生延王玢。肃宗每见王，则语左右曰：“我与王，兄弟中更相亲，外家皆关中贵族。”柳氏乃尚书右丞范之女，睦州刺史齐物之妹也。

肃宗在春宫，尝与诸王従玄宗诣太清宫。有龙见于殿之东梁，玄宗目之，顾问诸王：“有所见乎？”皆曰：“无之。”问太子，太子俯而未对。上问：“头在何处？”曰：“在东上。”抚之曰：“真我儿也。” 安禄山入觐，萧宗屡言其不臣之状，玄宗无言。一日，召太子诸王击球，太子潜欲以鞍马伤之。密谓太子曰：“吾非不疑，但此胡无尾，汝姑置之。” 政和公主，肃宗第三女也，降柳潭。肃宗宴于宫中，女优有弄假官戏，其绿衣秉简者，谓之参军妆。天宝末，蕃将阿布思伏法，其妻配掖庭，善为优，因使隶乐工。是日遂为假官之长。所为妆者，上及侍宴者笑乐。公主独俯首颦眉不视，上问其故，公主遂谏曰：“禁中侍女不少，何必须得此人使阿布思真逆人也，其妻亦同刑人，不合近至尊之座。若果冤横，又岂忍使其妻与群优杂处为笑谑之具哉妾虽至愚，深以为不可。”上亦悯恻，遂罢戏，而免阿布思之妻。由是贤重公主。

代宗独孤妃薨，赠贞懿皇后，将葬。尚父汾阳王在邠州，以其子尚主之故，欲致祭。遍问诸従事，皆云；“自古无人臣祭皇后之仪。”汾阳曰：“此事须得柳侍御裁之。”时予外伯祖殿中侍御史，掌汾阳书记，奉使在京，即以书急召之。既至，汾阳迎笑曰：“有切事，须藉侍御为之。”遂说祭事。殿中君初亦对如诸人，既而曰：“礼缘人情，令公勋德不同常人，且又为国姻戚，自令公始，亦谓得宜。”汾阳曰：“正合子仪本意。”殿中君草祭文，其官衔之首称：“驸马都尉郭暧父。”其中叙特恩许致祭之意，辞简礼备，汾阳览之大喜。其文列于左：

维某年月日，驸马都尉郭暧父，关内河东副元帅、司徒、兼中书令、汾阳郡王臣子仪，谨遣上都进奏院官傅涛，敢昭告于贞懿皇后行宫：伏惟德曜坤灵，明齐月魄，母仪万国，化洽六宫，光辅圣人，赞成阴教，载荣史策，式播讴谣。奄违圣日，上仙灵界，遐迩痛愤，宫闱哀慕。臣幸忝诸亲，男尚贵主，天人之美，鞠育所钟，姻戚光荣，宗族咸戴。今园陵礼备，祖载及期，臣限守方镇，不获陪侍行宫，瞻望灵驾，不胜摧慕。伏荷皇恩，眷以国戚。许申祭礼，超越等夷，古今所绝，独开圣造，无任惶恐铭戴之至。谨献牲牢庶羞之奠。尚飨！
